proposals.title: Impairment of Flow-Mediated, Nitric Oxide-Dependent Dilation of the Frontoparietal Neocortex in Murine Models of Hypertension and Atherosclerosis: A Mechanism for Vascular Cognitive Impairment
proposals.motivation: Flow-mediated (FN) vasodilation is a critical neurovascular coupling mechanism ensuring adequate cerebral blood flow to active brain regions. Chronic vascular diseases like hypertension and atherosclerosis are leading risk factors for vascular cognitive impairment (VCI) and dementia, but the specific impact of these conditions on the NO-dependent FN pathway in the neocortex remains poorly characterized. Understanding this link is essential to define the vascular pathophysiology underlying cognitive decline.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the nitric oxide (NO)-dependent component of FN vasodilation in the frontoparietal neocortex is selectively impaired in mouse models of chronic hypertension and atherosclerosis, and that this impairment correlates with deficits in working memory and cortical network function.
proposals.method: We will use in vivo two-photon laser scanning microscopy through a chronic cranial window in awake, head-fixed mice (C57BL/6J background) to measure cortical arteriolar diameter changes in response to contralateral whisker stimulation. We will compare wild-type controls to the angiotensin II-induced hypertensive model and the ApoE-/- model on a high-fat diet (atherosclerosis). Pharmacological blockade with L-NAME will isolate the NO-dependent component. Simultaneous laser Doppler flowmetry will measure bulk CBF changes. Behavioral correlation will be assessed using the Y-maze spontaneous alternation test. Data analysis will involve custom algorithms for vessel diameter tracking (MATLAB-based) and statistical parametric mapping of hemodynamic responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly link specific deficits in a fundamental neurovascular signaling pathway (NO-dependent FN dilation) to two major systemic vascular disease models, providing a mechanistic explanation for their contribution to VCI. It will establish a quantitative, in vivo framework for assessing cortical microvascular health in disease, identifying a potential therapeutic target for preserving cognitive function.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has shown generalized cerebrovascular dysfunction in disease models, this study uniquely focuses on the high-resolution, functional assessment of the NO-dependent FN pathway in the neocortex of awake animals, directly connecting a specific molecular vasodilatory mechanism to cognitive outcomes in hypertension and atherosclerosis.

proposals.title: Cerebellar Fastigial Nucleus Stimulation Augments Cerebral Perfusion and Reduces Infarct Volume via Nitric Oxide-Dependent Pathways in a Mouse Model of Focal Ischemia
proposals.motivation: Post-stroke cognitive impairment and dementia (Vascular Cognitive Impairment) are major consequences of cerebral ischemia, driven by inadequate cerebral blood flow (CBF) and perfusion deficits. Pre-2026 work by Iadecola and others has established the critical role of neurovascular coupling and endothelial nitric oxide (NO) in maintaining CBF. Electrical stimulation of the cerebellar fastigial nucleus (FN) is a known pre-conditioning stimulus that induces long-lasting, NO-dependent global cerebral vasodilation, but its therapeutic potential *after* stroke onset to salvage the ischemic penumbra remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that FN stimulation applied after the induction of focal cerebral ischemia will improve perfusion in the ischemic penumbra via an endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS)-dependent mechanism, leading to reduced infarct volume and improved functional outcomes.
proposals.method: We will use a well-established C57BL/6 mouse model of transient middle cerebral artery occlusion (tMCAO; 60 min occlusion). Bipolar electrodes will be stereotaxically implanted in the FN. Following tMCAO, animals will be randomized to receive either FN stimulation (50 µA, 0.5 ms pulses, 50 Hz for 1 hour) or sham stimulation. Cerebral perfusion will be quantified in real-time using laser speckle contrast imaging (LSCI) over the ipsilateral hemisphere. Infarct volume will be assessed at 72 hours using TTC staining. To test the NO-dependence, parallel experiments will be conducted in eNOS knockout mice and using pharmacological NOS inhibition (L-NAME). Behavioral outcomes will be evaluated using the adhesive removal and cylinder tests.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly test a novel neuromodulatory strategy to enhance post-stroke perfusion, bridging the gap between neurovascular physiology and acute stroke therapy. It will provide mechanistic insight into the role of central autonomic pathways and NO signaling in penumbral salvage. The findings could establish FN stimulation as a potential non-pharmacological adjunct to reperfusion therapies for mitigating vascular cognitive impairment.
proposals.novelty: While FN stimulation is known for its pre-conditioning effects, its application as a *post-ischemic* therapeutic intervention to actively improve penumbral perfusion via NO is novel and untested. This shifts the paradigm from prophylactic neuroprotection to acute, physiology-based rescue.

proposals.title: Direct Neurovascular Signaling Independent of Synaptic Transmission: A Two-Photon Uncaging and Circuit-Silencing Approach
proposals.motivation: Neurovascular coupling (NVC) is fundamental to brain health, and its dysfunction is a core feature of vascular cognitive impairment and dementia. A critical unresolved question is whether direct activation of neurons or astrocytes, without engaging synaptic transmission and network activity, is sufficient to evoke hemodynamic responses. Resolving this is essential for understanding the basic signaling units of NVC and for identifying precise therapeutic targets for vascular-based cognitive disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that direct, extrasynaptic activation of neuronal or astrocytic glutamate receptors, in the complete absence of functional synaptic vesicle release, is sufficient to trigger local vasodilation and increased cerebral blood flow.
proposals.method: We will combine targeted pharmacological blockade of synaptic vesicle release (e.g., tetanus toxin, botulinum neurotoxin) with two-photon glutamate uncaging to selectively activate extrasynaptic NMDA or metabotropic glutamate receptors on genetically identified pyramidal neurons or astrocytes in mouse somatosensory cortex. Hemodynamic responses (vessel diameter, red blood cell flux) will be measured via two-photon microscopy. A complementary optogenetic approach will use Channelrhodopsin-2 activation of a target neuron while rapidly inhibiting its postsynaptic partners via Archaerhodopsin to isolate direct from synaptically propagated vascular signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively establish whether synaptic transmission is an absolute requirement for functional hyperemia, isolating the direct vascular signaling capacity of neurons and astrocytes. It will provide a foundational framework for understanding which cellular pathways are disrupted in models of neurovascular aging and disease, such as hypertensive and ApoE4 models studied in our lab.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies manipulate network activity, this proposal uniquely combines precise subcellular stimulation with global synaptic silencing to interrogate synapse-bypassing signaling pathways, a direct and previously untested approach to decouple vascular responses from synaptic transmission.

proposals.title: Dissociating Synaptic and Non-Synaptic Mechanisms in Neurovascular Coupling: A Direct Optogenetic and Chemogenetic Investigation
proposals.motivation: Neurovascular coupling (NVC), the tight link between neuronal activity and cerebral blood flow, is fundamental for brain health and cognition. Its dysfunction is a core feature of vascular cognitive impairment and Alzheimer's disease. While synaptic glutamate release is considered the primary driver of NVC, it remains unknown whether direct, non-synaptic activation of neurons or astrocytes is sufficient to evoke hemodynamic responses, a critical question for understanding the basic signaling architecture of the brain's vascular supply.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that direct, non-synaptic activation of specific neuronal populations or astrocytes, using optogenetic and chemogenetic tools, will be insufficient to evoke robust, localized cerebral blood flow increases, demonstrating that synaptic transmission is a mandatory step for functional hyperemia.
proposals.method: We will employ transgenic mice (e.g., Thy1-ChR2 for pyramidal neurons, GFAP-ChR2 for astrocytes) and viral vectors to express channelrhodopsin-2 (ChR2) or the excitatory DREADD hM3Dq in cortical neurons and astrocytes. Using two-photon microscopy and laser Doppler flowmetry in awake, head-fixed mice, we will measure cerebral blood flow changes in response to: 1) direct optogenetic stimulation of neuronal somata/axons, bypassing synaptic inputs, and 2) chemogenetic (CNO) activation of astrocytes. These will be compared to sensory-evoked (whisker stimulation) and synaptically-driven (optogenetic stimulation of presynaptic afferents) hyperemia as positive controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively test the necessity of synaptic transmission for NVC, clarifying a fundamental principle of brain physiology. It will distinguish the roles of action potential firing per se from synaptic signaling in vascular control, with implications for interpreting fMRI signals and understanding how NVC fails in neurodegenerative and cerebrovascular diseases.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has correlated synaptic activity with blood flow, no study has systematically isolated and tested the sufficiency of non-synaptic cellular activation in vivo. Using precise cell-type-specific actuators to bypass synapses directly addresses this causal gap.

proposals.title: Ablating Astrocyte Calcium Signaling to Test its Obligatory Role in Neurovascular Coupling: A Direct Test of the Relay Hypothesis
proposals.motivation: Neurovascular coupling (NVC) is fundamental for brain health, and its dysfunction is a key mechanism in vascular cognitive impairment and dementia. The prevailing model posits astrocytes as essential intermediaries, relaying signals from active neurons to blood vessels. However, direct neuronal-to-vascular signaling pathways also exist, creating a critical debate about the necessity of the astrocytic relay for functional hyperemia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that astrocyte calcium signaling, specifically via the IP3R2 pathway, is not an obligatory relay for functional hyperemia, and that neurons can initiate vasodilation directly, with astrocytes playing a modulatory role.
proposals.method: We will use in vivo two-photon microscopy and laser Doppler flowmetry in awake, head-fixed mice to measure cerebral blood flow (CBF) responses in somatosensory cortex during whisker stimulation. We will employ IP3R2 global knockout mice and, for greater specificity, use local viral delivery of Gi-DREADDs (hM4Di) or designer receptors exclusively activated by designer drugs (DREADDs) in astrocytes to chemogenetically inhibit their signaling. This targeted ablation/inhibition will be combined with direct optogenetic stimulation of cortical neurons to isolate the vascular response from pure neuronal activation.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively test the necessity of astrocyte calcium signaling for NVC, resolving a central controversy in cerebrovascular physiology. It will establish whether astrocytes are a critical bottleneck or a parallel modulatory system, with direct implications for understanding how NVC fails in hypertension, aging, and neurodegenerative diseases.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct analogy to classic ablation studies in cardiac pacemaker research, applying a definitive 'necessity test' to the astrocyte relay hypothesis using precise, cell-type-specific inhibition in vivo during behaviorally relevant neural activation.

proposals.title: Direct Neuronal Control of Functional Hyperemia: Testing the Necessity of Astrocytic Intermediaries in the Neurovascular Coupling Cascade
proposals.motivation: Neurovascular coupling (NVC), the tight link between neuronal activity and local cerebral blood flow, is fundamental for brain health and is disrupted in vascular cognitive impairment and Alzheimer's disease. The canonical model posits that synaptic glutamate activates astrocytic metabotropic receptors, triggering vasoactive signal release. However, direct neuronal release of vasodilators like nitric oxide or arachidonic acid derivatives could bypass astrocytes, a pathway whose physiological significance remains unresolved. Clarifying this hierarchy is critical for understanding the primary cellular locus of NVC failure in dementia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that astrocytes, while capable of modulating blood flow, are not obligatory intermediaries for activity-dependent hyperemia; instead, neurons can generate sufficient vasoactive signals directly to drive functional hyperemia.
proposals.method: We will use in vivo two-photon microscopy in awake, head-fixed transgenic mice (e.g., Thy1-GCaMP6f for neuronal Ca2+, GFAP-GCaMP6f for astrocytic Ca2+, and Texas Red dextran for vessel diameter) to simultaneously monitor neuronal activity, astrocytic endfoot Ca2+, and arteriolar dilation in somatosensory cortex during whisker stimulation. We will employ cell-type-specific, inducible genetic tools (AAV-DIO-ArchT or AAV-DIO-hM4Di) to selectively and reversibly inhibit astrocytic Ca2+ signaling or vesicular release (via dnSNARE expression) in the imaging field, testing if hyperemia persists. Pharmacological blockade of neuronal nitric oxide synthase (nNOS) and cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) pathways will be combined with astrocyte inhibition to dissect signal origins.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively test the necessity of the astrocyte in the NVC cascade, establishing a more precise cellular model of functional hyperemia. It will identify which vasoactive pathways are neuronally autonomous versus astrocyte-dependent, providing specific cellular targets for rescuing neurovascular dysfunction in models of hypertension and Alzheimer's disease pioneered by the Iadecola lab.
proposals.novelty: While prior work shows astrocytes can influence vessels, this proposal directly tests if they are *necessary* for evoked hyperemia in vivo, using simultaneous tripartite imaging and cell-specific perturbation to dissect the signaling hierarchy, moving beyond correlation to causation.

proposals.title: Differential contributions of ionotropic and metabotropic glutamate receptors to neurovascular coupling in a mouse model of vascular cognitive impairment
proposals.motivation: Neurovascular coupling (NVC), the process linking neuronal activity to increased cerebral blood flow, is a critical mechanism for brain health and is impaired in vascular cognitive impairment (VCI) and Alzheimer's disease. While glutamate is the primary excitatory neurotransmitter driving NVC, the specific receptor subtypes (AMPA, NMDA, mGluR) mediating this response, particularly in disease states, remain ambiguous. Pre-2026 work from the Iadecola lab has established the importance of vascular dysfunction in cognitive decline, but a precise pharmacological dissection of the glutamatergic pathway is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a mouse model of VCI (e.g., bilateral common carotid artery stenosis), the contribution of specific glutamate receptor subtypes to functional hyperemia is altered, with a pathological shift towards NMDA receptor-dominant signaling that contributes to impaired hemodynamics and cognitive deficits.
proposals.method: We will use in vivo two-photon laser scanning microscopy in awake, head-fixed C57BL/6 mice (and a VCI model) to simultaneously measure neuronal Ca2+ (GCaMP6f) and arteriolar diameter in the somatosensory cortex during whisker stimulation. We will systemically administer selective, brain-penetrant antagonists for AMPA (NBQX), NMDA (MK-801), and group I mGluRs (MTEP) in a within-subject crossover design. The primary outcome is the attenuation of the stimulus-evoked arteriolar dilation. Data will be analyzed using custom MATLAB routines for time-series alignment of Ca2+ transients and vasodilation, with statistical comparison of drug effects on NVC gain.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, in vivo mapping of glutamatergic receptor-specific pathways in NVC within a VCI context. It will identify which receptor(s) are viable therapeutic targets for restoring functional hyperemia. The findings will directly inform the pathobiology of neurovascular aging and offer a mechanistic link between vascular glutamate signaling and cognitive symptoms.
proposals.novelty: Previous NVC studies often used broad glutamate receptor blockers or focused on healthy tissue. This proposal uniquely combines precise pharmacological dissection with high-resolution in vivo imaging in a disease-relevant model, testing the non-obvious premise that VCI pathophysiology involves a specific re-routing of glutamatergic NVC signaling.

proposals.title: Parvalbumin-positive interneuron-mediated inhibition of neurovascular coupling in the cerebellar cortex
proposals.motivation: Neurovascular coupling (NVC), the tight link between neuronal activity and local cerebral blood flow, is fundamental for brain health and is disrupted in vascular cognitive impairment and dementia. While excitatory neuron-driven vasodilation is well-studied, the role of inhibitory interneurons in actively modulating NVC remains poorly understood. Determining if specific interneurons can suppress or shape the hemodynamic response is critical for a complete model of brain energy supply and for understanding pathologies where inhibitory circuits are altered.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that activation of parvalbumin-positive (PV+) inhibitory interneurons in the cerebellar molecular layer suppresses the local blood flow increase typically evoked by parallel fiber stimulation, thereby providing a dynamic inhibitory control mechanism for neurovascular coupling.
proposals.method: We will use in vivo two-photon microscopy in anesthetized transgenic mice (PV-Cre x Ai32) to optogenetically stimulate PV+ interneurons in Crus I/II of the cerebellum, while simultaneously imaging arteriolar diameter and red blood cell flux. In parallel experiments, we will combine parallel fiber stimulation (via a concentric bipolar electrode) with timed optogenetic activation or chemogenetic inhibition (using DREADDs in PV-Cre mice) of PV+ interneurons to measure their effect on the evoked hemodynamic response. Laser Doppler flowmetry will provide complementary bulk flow measurements. All data will be analyzed with custom MATLAB routines for time-series alignment and statistical comparison of vascular parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct causal link between a defined inhibitory cell type and the suppression of functional hyperemia, adding a new layer of regulatory complexity to the neurovascular unit. It will provide a mechanistic framework for how inhibitory-excitatory balance influences blood flow, with implications for understanding dysfunctional NVC in conditions like hypertension and Alzheimer's disease, where interneuron deficits are reported.
proposals.novelty: While prior work from the Iadecola lab and others has focused on excitatory drivers and pericyte/endothelial mechanisms of NVC, the active inhibitory modulation of blood flow by a genetically identified interneuron population is a novel and non-obvious concept. This moves beyond viewing interneurons as mere modulators of neural activity to positioning them as direct regulators of vascular function.

proposals.title: Causal mapping of the neurovascular unit: Simultaneous two-photon imaging and targeted microdialysis to resolve the spatiotemporal dynamics of vasoactive signaling in the awake mouse cortex
proposals.motivation: Neurovascular coupling (NVC) is fundamental for brain health, and its dysfunction is a critical early event in vascular cognitive impairment and Alzheimer's disease. However, a major pre-2026 knowledge gap is the inability to directly link the dynamic release of specific vasoactive molecules in the extracellular space with the cellular and capillary-level hemodynamic responses they are proposed to drive, limiting causal understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the precise spatiotemporal alignment of extracellular chemical sampling with cellular-resolution imaging will reveal distinct, stimulus-specific patterns of vasoactive molecule release (e.g., glutamate, K+, PGE2) that causally govern the initiation and propagation of functional hyperemia at the capillary level.
proposals.method: We will integrate chronic cranial window preparation in awake, head-fixed mice with a custom-built, miniaturized microdialysis probe positioned under the objective for simultaneous two-photon imaging. The probe's membrane will be precisely aligned to the imaging field (layer 2/3 somatosensory cortex). We will perform concurrent imaging of capillary red blood cell flux (via Texas Red dextran) and neuronal/astrocytic Ca2+ (GCaMP6) during whisker stimulation, while collecting dialysate for targeted analysis via ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography and mass spectrometry. Pharmacological agents (e.g., P2Y12 receptor antagonists, COX inhibitors) will be delivered via reverse dialysis to test causal pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, temporally resolved chemical map of the neurovascular unit during functional activation, establishing causal links between specific signaling pathways and capillary hemodynamics. It will directly test prevailing models of NVC (e.g., the role of astrocyte-derived prostaglandins vs. neuronal K+) and identify which pathways are vulnerable in models of vascular aging and hypertension.
proposals.novelty: The novel fusion of these established techniques creates a closed-loop, causal experimental platform where the chemical environment can be both measured and manipulated in real time, with cellular and capillary resolution, which is not possible with either method alone.

proposals.title: A Biophysical Model of the Neurovascular Unit to Predict Functional Hyperemia Deficits in Aging and APOE4 Genotype
proposals.motivation: Neurovascular coupling (NVC), the tight link between synaptic activity and local cerebral blood flow, is a critical pillar of brain health and is disrupted early in vascular cognitive impairment and Alzheimer's disease. Pre-2026 work, including Dr. Iadecola's, has identified key cellular players (e.g., astrocytes, pericytes, endothelial cells) and signaling pathways (e.g., purinergic P2Y12 receptors), but a predictive, quantitative framework integrating these components is lacking. This gap hinders our ability to mechanistically link specific molecular deficits to the hemodynamic dysregulation observed in aging and dementia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a computational model integrating synaptic glutamate release, astrocytic calcium dynamics, pericyte contractility, and arteriolar diameter change will accurately simulate the NVC response and predict how aging and the APOE4 genotype shift the model's parameters to produce the blunted functional hyperemia characteristic of early cognitive decline.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-compartment, ordinary differential equation model using established biophysical formalisms (e.g., Hodgkin-Huxley-type equations for cellular activation, Michaelis-Menten kinetics for metabolism). The model architecture will explicitly include neuronal glutamate release, astrocytic endfoot IP3-mediated Ca2+ signaling, pericyte membrane potential and contractile state, and smooth muscle cell tone. Key parameters (e.g., ATP production rate, K+ channel conductance, P2Y12 receptor sensitivity) will be informed by and fitted to in vivo two-photon microscopy data of cortical blood flow and Ca2+ dynamics in awake, behaving mice (wild-type, aged, and APOE4 models). Model validation will involve predicting the hemodynamic response to novel sensory or working memory tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated, testable biophysical model of the neurovascular unit, moving the field from a descriptive to a predictive understanding of NVC. It will generate specific, quantifiable predictions about which molecular pathways are most vulnerable in aging and APOE4 carriers, offering novel targets for therapeutic intervention in vascular cognitive impairment.
proposals.novelty: While prior models have focused on isolated aspects of blood flow regulation, our model's novelty lies in its direct integration of the specific cell types and purinergic signaling pathways implicated by recent experimental work from our lab and others, creating a direct bridge between molecular pathophysiology and systems-level hemodynamic dysfunction.

proposals.title: Selective Activation of Somatostatin Interneurons Reduces Baseline Cerebral Blood Flow in the Mouse Cortex
proposals.motivation: Neurovascular coupling (NVC) research has focused on how excitatory neuronal activity increases local blood flow. However, the vascular impact of inhibitory interneurons, which are critical for network balance and are dysfunctional in dementia, is poorly understood. Given that baseline cerebral blood flow (CBF) is reduced in vascular cognitive impairment and Alzheimer's disease, understanding whether inhibitory circuits actively suppress flow is crucial for deciphering the vascular pathophysiology of cognitive decline.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that selective optogenetic activation of somatostatin-positive (SST+) inhibitory interneurons in the somatosensory cortex will lead to a measurable decrease in baseline CBF, mediated by specific vasoconstrictive signaling pathways.
proposals.method: We will use SST-Cre mice crossed with Ai32 (ChR2) mice for cell-type-specific optogenetic activation. Baseline CBF will be measured in the whisker barrel cortex using laser speckle contrast imaging (LSCI) in awake, head-fixed mice. We will deliver 470nm light pulses (5-20Hz) to activate SST+ interneurons and quantify the CBF response. Pharmacological interventions (e.g., local application of the P2Y12 receptor antagonist ticagrelor or a nitric oxide synthase inhibitor) will dissect the vascular signaling mechanisms. Parallel local field potential recordings will confirm neural activation and network inhibition.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct causal link between inhibitory interneuron activity and negative regulation of cerebral blood flow, a novel component of the neurovascular unit. It will identify specific molecular mediators of this inhibitory neurovascular coupling, providing new mechanistic insights into how dysregulated inhibition might contribute to chronic hypoperfusion in dementia.
proposals.novelty: While prior work shows excitatory-driven hyperemia, the concept that inhibitory neurons can actively reduce baseline flow is unexplored. This shifts the paradigm from viewing baseline flow as passively maintained to being dynamically suppressed by local inhibitory tone.

proposals.title: Isoform-Independent Contribution of Neuronal, Endothelial, and Inducible Nitric Oxide Synthase to Post-Ischemic Neurovascular Dysfunction and Cognitive Decline
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that nitric oxide (NO) signaling, mediated by distinct NOS isoforms, is critically involved in cerebral ischemia and vascular cognitive impairment. However, the field is dominated by a paradigm attributing specific protective (eNOS) or damaging (nNOS, iNOS) roles to individual isoforms, creating a fragmented view. A comprehensive, simultaneous assessment of all three isoforms in a unified model of post-stroke vascular dysfunction is lacking, hindering the development of effective therapeutic strategies targeting the neurovascular unit.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that following cerebral ischemia, all three NOS isoforms (nNOS, eNOS, iNOS) contribute equally to the pathogenesis of chronic neurovascular dysfunction and associated cognitive deficits, rather than having opposing, isoform-specific effects.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged approach using transgenic mouse models (nNOS-/-, eNOS-/-, iNOS-/-) and a novel triple-pharmacological inhibition strategy targeting all isoforms simultaneously. Mice will undergo transient middle cerebral artery occlusion (tMCAO). Chronic post-ischemic neurovascular function will be assessed longitudinally using laser speckle contrast imaging for cerebral blood flow (CBF) and two-photon microscopy in awake, head-fixed Thy1-GCaMP6f mice to measure neurovascular coupling. Cognitive function will be evaluated using the Barnes maze and novel object recognition tests. Data will be analyzed using mixed-effects models to compare the temporal progression of deficits across genotypes and treatment groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, comparative evidence challenging the isoform-centric view of NO in stroke recovery. It will establish a new framework where global dysregulation of NO bioavailability, rather than the action of a single isoform, drives chronic vascular dysfunction. This could redirect therapeutic efforts from isoform-selective inhibition towards modulating the overall redox balance of the neurovascular niche.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly testing the non-intuitive concept of equal pathogenic contribution from all NOS isoforms—including the canonically protective eNOS—in the same model, and employing a concurrent triple-inhibition strategy, which has not been explored pre-2026 for chronic post-ischemic outcomes.

proposals.title: Defining the Therapeutic Window for Selective nNOS Inhibition to Restore Post-Ischemic Neurovascular Coupling and Cognitive Function
proposals.motivation: Cerebral ischemia disrupts neurovascular coupling (NVC), a key mechanism linking neural activity to blood flow, contributing to vascular cognitive impairment (VCI). Pre-2026 work shows that excess nitric oxide (NO) from neuronal NOS (nNOS) post-ischemia impairs vasoreactivity, but the time course of this dysfunction and the optimal window for therapeutic intervention are unknown. Determining this is critical for translating NOS inhibition from a neuroprotective concept into a viable strategy for preserving cognitive health after stroke.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a defined, delayed time window exists after focal cerebral ischemia during which selective nNOS inhibition can effectively restore NVC and prevent long-term cognitive deficits, with efficacy dependent on the post-ischemic inflammatory and vascular remodeling phases.
proposals.method: We will induce photothrombotic focal ischemia in C57BL/6 mice and treat cohorts with the selective nNOS inhibitor, 7-nitroindazole (7-NI), at 1, 3, 7, or 14 days post-ischemia. Longitudinal in vivo two-photon microscopy through cranial windows will assess cortical arteriolar diameter and red blood cell flux during whisker stimulation to quantify NVC recovery. Laser speckle contrast imaging will map cerebral blood flow. Behavioral tests (Novel Object Recognition, Y-maze) at 30 days will evaluate cognitive outcomes. Post-mortem analysis will correlate results with immunohistochemistry for nNOS, microglial activation (Iba1), and astrocytic end-feet (AQP4).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, longitudinal mapping of NVC recovery dynamics after focal ischemia and establish a precise therapeutic window for nNOS-targeted intervention. It will advance the field of vascular contributions to cognitive impairment by linking a specific vascular mechanism (dysfunctional NVC) to a time-sensitive treatment strategy, moving beyond acute neuroprotection to post-stroke cognitive rescue.
proposals.novelty: While NOS inhibition's neuroprotective role in the acute phase (<24h) is known, this proposal uniquely investigates its efficacy in a delayed, subacute-to-chronic phase specifically to restore cerebrovascular function, a novel approach for mitigating post-stroke dementia risk. It integrates Iadecola's expertise in NOS biology with advanced in vivo imaging of neurovascular dynamics over time.

proposals.title: Longitudinal fMRI Assessment of Cerebrovascular Reactivity Predicts Post-Stroke Cognitive Decline in a Mouse Model of Vascular Cognitive Impairment
proposals.motivation: Vascular cognitive impairment (VCI) is a major consequence of stroke, driven by impaired cerebrovascular reactivity (CVR) and neurovascular coupling. Pre-2026, the field lacks non-invasive, longitudinal biomarkers to track the evolution of CVR deficits after stroke and link them to specific cognitive domains. Establishing such a biomarker is critical for understanding the pathobiology of post-stroke VCI and for evaluating therapeutic interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longitudinal fMRI assessment of hypercapnia-induced CVR, performed serially after experimental stroke, will reveal a spatiotemporal pattern of impaired vascular reactivity that correlates with and predicts the progression of domain-specific cognitive deficits, particularly in memory and executive function.
proposals.method: We will use a murine photothrombotic stroke model. Serial fMRI (9.4T) with a hypercapnic challenge (5% CO2) will be performed at baseline, 1, 4, and 12 weeks post-stroke to map CVR via Blood Oxygenation Level-Dependent (BOLD) signal changes. CVR maps will be generated using a general linear model (GLM) with the end-tidal CO2 trace as a regressor. Concurrently, mice will undergo behavioral testing (Morris water maze, Y-maze, novel object recognition). Data will be analyzed using correlation and mixed-effects models to link regional CVR deficits to cognitive outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a non-invasive, longitudinal fMRI protocol for CVR assessment in a stroke model, providing a direct link between evolving vascular dysfunction and cognitive decline. It will identify critical time windows and brain regions where CVR loss most strongly predicts VCI, offering a translational biomarker for preclinical therapeutic evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While fMRI and hypercapnia are established, their combined longitudinal application to map the dynamic relationship between post-stroke CVR recovery and specific cognitive trajectories in a VCI model is novel. This approach moves beyond static, single-time-point assessments to capture the evolving vascular pathophysiology of dementia.

proposals.title: Targeting complementary vasodilatory pathways to restore functional hyperemia after ischemic challenge in a mouse model of vascular cognitive impairment
proposals.motivation: Cerebrovascular reactivity, particularly functional hyperemia, is critically impaired in vascular cognitive impairment (VCI) and after ischemic events, contributing to cognitive decline. Pre-2026 work from our lab and others shows that nitric oxide synthase (NOS) dysfunction is a key mediator of this impairment, but NOS inhibition alone does not fully restore the neurovascular coupling response, suggesting the involvement of other compromised pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the combined pharmacological inhibition of NOS and targeted activation of complementary vasodilatory pathways (specifically, prostaglandin and epoxyecosatrienoic acid signaling) will synergistically restore full cerebrovascular reactivity and functional hyperemia after a transient ischemic challenge in a mouse model of VCI.
proposals.method: We will use hypertensive ApoE4 mice, a model of VCI, subjected to a mild, bilateral common carotid artery stenosis to induce chronic hypoperfusion. Cerebrovascular reactivity will be assessed in vivo using two-photon laser scanning microscopy and laser Doppler flowmetry to measure cortical blood flow changes in response to whisker stimulation. Mice will be treated with: 1) a NOS inhibitor (L-NAME), 2) a COX-2 inhibitor (celecoxib) or a soluble epoxide hydrolase inhibitor (TPPU) to elevate EETs, and 3) their combination. Cognitive function will be assessed using the Morris water maze and novel object recognition tests.
proposals.contribution: This study will determine whether a multi-target vasoregulatory strategy is superior to single-pathway modulation for rescuing neurovascular coupling after ischemia. It will identify specific synergistic pathway interactions and provide a preclinical proof-of-concept for a novel therapeutic approach to mitigate post-ischemic cognitive deficits in VCI.
proposals.novelty: While NOS and other vasodilators have been studied individually, systematically testing their combined effect to overcome the multifaceted dysfunction in post-ischemic neurovascular coupling is novel. This approach moves beyond single-mechanism rescue to address the integrated failure of the neurovascular unit.

proposals.title: Neuronal Nitric Oxide Synthase Mediates Functional Hyperemia in the Cerebral Cortex: A Direct Test in a Murine Model of Vascular Cognitive Impairment
proposals.motivation: Functional neurovascular coupling (NVC), the process by which increased neuronal activity triggers local vasodilation to match blood flow to metabolic demand, is fundamental for healthy cognition and is impaired in vascular cognitive impairment (VCI) and Alzheimer's disease. While endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS) is a canonical regulator of vascular tone, its specific role in activity-driven, functional hyperemia remains debated, with evidence suggesting neuronal NOS (nNOS) may be the primary source of the initiating NO signal. Resolving this source is critical for understanding the pathophysiology of NVC failure in dementia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that nNOS-derived nitric oxide (NO), not eNOS-derived NO, is the primary mediator of functional hyperemia in the cerebral cortex, and that selective impairment of nNOS-mediated signaling contributes to the neurovascular uncoupling observed in hypertensive models of VCI.
proposals.method: We will use in vivo two-photon laser scanning microscopy in awake, head-fixed mice (C57BL/6J) to measure cerebral blood flow (CBF) in the somatosensory cortex via fluorescently labeled red blood cells and vessel diameter. Functional hyperemia will be evoked by contralateral whisker stimulation. We will employ pharmacological inhibition (7-NI for nNOS; L-NIO for eNOS) and genetic models (nNOS-/- and eNOS-/- mice) to dissect the NO source. These experiments will be repeated in a hypertensive, hyperhomocysteinemic mouse model of VCI to assess pathway-specific dysfunction. Data analysis will involve custom algorithms for vessel tracking and diameter quantification (using ImageJ/FIJI plugins) and statistical comparison of CBF response kinetics and magnitude across groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively identify the principal cellular source of NO for functional hyperemia, clarifying a long-standing controversy in neurovascular biology. It will establish whether nNOS-mediated signaling is a vulnerable pathway in hypertensive VCI, providing a novel mechanistic target for therapies aimed at restoring neurovascular coupling and cognitive function.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have implicated both eNOS and nNOS in CBF regulation, this proposal directly tests their relative contributions to the rapid, activity-evoked vasodilation of functional hyperemia in vivo, and uniquely links a specific NO source to the neurovascular pathology of a defined VCI model.

proposals.title: Impaired Functional Hyperemia Mediated by Fast-Neuronal (FN) Signaling in Mouse Models of Vascular Cognitive Impairment and Post-Stroke Dementia
proposals.motivation: Functional hyperemia, the coupling of neuronal activity to cerebral blood flow, is essential for brain health and cognition. Pre-2026 work from the Iadecola lab and others has established that aging and vascular risk factors impair neurovascular coupling (NVC), contributing to cognitive decline. However, the specific failure of the rapid, neuronally-driven (FN) component of the hemodynamic response in progressive neurodegenerative and post-ischemic conditions remains poorly characterized, limiting targeted therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that FN-mediated hyperemia is selectively and progressively impaired in mouse models of vascular cognitive impairment (VCI) and post-stroke dementia, and that this impairment correlates with the severity of working memory deficits.
proposals.method: We will use established mouse models: chronic cerebral hypoperfusion (bilateral carotid artery stenosis) for VCI and photothrombotic cortical stroke combined with hypertensive ApoE4 background for post-stroke dementia. In vivo two-photon laser scanning microscopy through a cranial window will be used to measure red blood cell flux and vessel diameter in the somatosensory cortex. FN-mediated hyperemia will be evoked by controlled whisker stimulation and quantified by the latency, amplitude, and spatial spread of the hemodynamic response. These parameters will be correlated with behavioral performance on the Y-maze and novel object recognition tests at multiple time points post-injury.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, longitudinal evidence linking the loss of specific FN-mediated hemodynamic signaling to cognitive decline in VCI and post-stroke dementia models. It will identify a precise vascular pathophysiology that can be targeted to restore NVC, informing the development of therapies aimed at preserving cognition in cerebrovascular disease.
proposals.novelty: While general NVC deficits are known, this proposal focuses specifically on the fast, neuronally-initiated component (FN response) in two distinct but clinically convergent dementia models, linking its dynamic impairment directly to progressive cognitive metrics rather than a single time point.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Temporal and Spatial Coupling of Neurovascular and Neurometabolic Responses in the Activated Cerebellum
proposals.motivation: The neurovascular unit (NVU) ensures precise coupling of cerebral blood flow (CBF) to neuronal activity, a process critical for brain health and cognition. Pre-2026 work by Iadecola and others shows that NVU dysfunction underpins vascular cognitive impairment and dementia. However, the precise temporal and quantitative relationship between CBF and glucose utilization (CMRglc) during specific cerebellar tasks remains poorly defined, limiting our understanding of regional energy demands in brain activation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during cerebellar activation, the increase in regional CBF is tightly coupled to a proportional increase in CMRglc, but with a measurable temporal delay reflecting metabolic demand following neural activity, and that this coupling is impaired in a mouse model of vascular aging.
proposals.method: We will use in vivo two-photon laser scanning microscopy in awake, head-fixed Thy1-GCaMP6f mice to image neuronal calcium activity in cerebellar Crus I/II during a forelimb reaching task. Simultaneously, we will measure CBF via laser speckle contrast imaging (LSCI) in the same field. Glucose utilization will be quantified using the fluorescent glucose analog 2-NBDG, administered intravenously, with its uptake kinetics measured via two-photon microscopy. Data will be analyzed using custom algorithms in MATLAB for time-series correlation (cross-correlation, Granger causality) and spatial mapping of CBF and 2-NBDG signal co-localization.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, high-resolution quantification of the spatiotemporal coupling between blood flow and glucose metabolism in the activated cerebellum. It will establish normative benchmarks for neurovascular-metabolic coupling, which can be used to assess dysfunction in models of vascular aging and dementia, directly linking to Dr. Iadecola's work on vascular cognitive impairment.
proposals.novelty: While CBF-metabolism coupling is well-studied in the cortex, applying simultaneous, high-resolution in vivo imaging of neural activity, hemodynamics, and glucose analog uptake to the cerebellum during a defined motor task is novel. This approach moves beyond correlative BOLD-fMRI studies to establish direct causal and temporal links within the cerebellar NVU.

proposals.title: Single-Vessel Cerebrovascular Dysfunction in a Mouse Model of Vascular Cognitive Impairment: High-Resolution Imaging of Cerebellar Hemodynamics During Whisker Stimulation
proposals.motivation: Neurovascular coupling (NVC), the precise regulation of cerebral blood flow to active neurons, is a critical pillar of brain health and cognitive function. Pre-2026 work by Iadecola and others has established that NVC is globally impaired in models of vascular cognitive impairment (VCI) and dementia, but the precise locus of failure—whether in penetrating arterioles, capillaries, or both—remains unclear, especially in the understudied cerebellum. This gap limits targeted therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a mouse model of VCI (e.g., chronic cerebral hypoperfusion or hypertensive ApoE4), the impairment of functional hyperemia originates at the level of capillary pericytes and/or precapillary arterioles in the cerebellum, leading to heterogeneous and inefficient perfusion during neural activation.
proposals.method: We will use in vivo two-photon laser scanning microscopy in awake, head-fixed mice expressing fluorescent reporters in endothelial cells (Tie2-GFP) and pericytes (NG2-DsRed). A chronic cranial window will be implanted over the cerebellar crus I/II region. Mice will undergo unilateral whisker pad stimulation. We will measure red blood cell (RBC) velocity and flux in individual penetrating arterioles, precapillary arterioles, and capillaries using line-scan analysis and particle image velocimetry algorithms. Simultaneous local field potential recordings will confirm neural activation. Comparisons will be made between VCI models and wild-type controls.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution, single-vessel map of functional hyperemia in the healthy and diseased cerebellum. It will directly identify which segment of the microvasculature is primarily responsible for NVC deficits in VCI. The findings will bridge the gap between whole-brain flow measurements and cellular pathophysiology, offering a refined biomarker and potential cellular target (e.g., pericytes) for rescuing cognitive decline.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined cortical NVC at the single-vessel level, applying this granular approach to the cerebellum in a VCI context is novel. Furthermore, integrating simultaneous electrophysiology with sub-capillary hemodynamic measurements in an awake disease model will reveal the direct relationship between neural firing and microvascular dysfunction.

proposals.title: Cerebellar Crus II Stimulation Modulates Thalamocortical Neurovascular Coupling: A Network Mechanism for Vascular Cognitive Impairment
proposals.motivation: Vascular contributions to cognitive impairment and dementia (VCID) are increasingly recognized, but the role of long-range cerebrovascular network dysregulation is poorly understood. Pre-2026 work by Iadecola and others highlights the cerebellum's emerging role in cognition and its connectivity to forebrain regions like the thalamus, yet how cerebellar activity influences blood flow in these connected networks, and how this is disrupted in vascular disease, remains unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that optogenetic stimulation of cerebellar crus II neurons will elicit region-specific changes in cerebral blood flow (CBF) in anatomically connected thalamic nuclei, and that this long-range neurovascular coupling will be impaired in a mouse model of chronic cerebral hypoperfusion, contributing to cognitive deficits.
proposals.method: We will use Thy1-ChR2 mice for cell-type-specific optogenetic stimulation of cerebellar crus II. Concurrently, we will perform whole-brain, high-resolution laser speckle contrast imaging (LSCI) to map CBF changes. Functional connectivity and CBF responses will be quantified in target regions (e.g., thalamic nuclei, prefrontal cortex) using pre-2026 analytical pipelines (e.g., seed-based correlation, general linear modeling). These experiments will be repeated in a bilateral common carotid artery stenosis (BCAS) mouse model of chronic hypoperfusion and in aged ApoE4 mice to assess disease-related disruption.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel model of long-range, network-level neurovascular coupling, directly linking cerebellar activity to forebrain hemodynamics. It will identify specific cerebrovascular pathways vulnerable to hypoperfusion, providing a mechanistic framework for how remote vascular dysregulation contributes to VCID beyond local pathology.
proposals.novelty: While local neurovascular coupling is well-studied, investigating the cerebellum as a driver of hemodynamic changes in distant cognitive hubs is novel. Integrating optogenetics with whole-brain CBF mapping to dissect network-level vascular physiology in disease models moves beyond correlative clinical studies.

proposals.title: Quantitative Inversion of Neurovascular Coupling: A Predictive Model of Cerebellar Neuronal Activity from Hemodynamic Flow Signatures
proposals.motivation: The neurovascular unit is central to brain health, and its dysfunction underpins vascular cognitive impairment and dementia. While the forward relationship—neural activity driving blood flow—is well-studied, the inverse problem of precisely quantifying neural activity from hemodynamic signals remains unsolved, particularly for the cerebellum, a region critical for motor and cognitive functions. Solving this inverse problem would provide a powerful, non-invasive tool for assessing circuit-specific dysfunction in cerebrovascular diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the magnitude of task-evoked cerebellar neuronal activity can be accurately inferred from high-resolution cerebral blood flow (CBF) measurements by modeling the nonlinear, region-specific properties of neurovascular coupling.
proposals.method: We will use a multi-modal approach in a mouse model, combining two-photon imaging of cerebellar CBF with simultaneous fiber photometry of GCamP8 calcium signals as a ground-truth measure of population neuronal activity. We will develop a predictive model using a physics-informed neural network (PINN) architecture. This model will integrate known biophysical constraints of neurovascular coupling (e.g., hemodynamic response functions, vascular reactivity) with the high-dimensional spatiotemporal flow data to learn a robust inverse mapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first validated, quantitative model for inferring cerebellar neural activity from blood flow, moving beyond qualitative correlation. It will establish a novel computational framework for analyzing neurovascular coupling that can be adapted to other brain regions. The findings will provide a critical new methodology for assessing neural circuit integrity in models of hypertension, aging, and Alzheimer's disease pathology, directly relevant to vascular cognitive impairment.
proposals.novelty: Existing work primarily correlates flow and activity; here we develop a predictive, quantitative model for the cerebellum, a region with distinct vascular architecture and coupling properties. The application of a physics-informed machine learning approach to this inverse problem in vivo is a significant methodological advance over traditional linear deconvolution techniques.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Vascular Mechanisms of L-NAME: Off-Target Effects on Cerebrovascular CO2 Reactivity Beyond NOS Inhibition
proposals.motivation: L-NAME is a cornerstone pharmacological tool for studying nitric oxide (NO) signaling in cerebral hemodynamics and neurovascular coupling. Its use underpins models of vascular cognitive impairment and dementia. However, its presumed specificity for endothelial NOS is critical; if L-NAME has significant off-target vascular effects, decades of foundational research on NO in cerebral blood flow regulation and CO2 reactivity may require reinterpretation, impacting our understanding of vascular contributions to cognitive disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that L-NAME attenuates cerebrovascular CO2 reactivity not solely via NOS inhibition, but also through off-target mechanisms affecting vascular smooth muscle or endothelial signaling pathways independent of NO synthesis.
proposals.method: We will employ laser Doppler flowmetry and two-photon microscopy in awake, head-fixed mice (C57BL/6J) to measure cortical cerebral blood flow (CBF) responses to hypercapnia (5-10% CO2) before and after L-NAME administration. To isolate off-target effects, we will compare L-NAME to a selective nNOS inhibitor (e.g., S-Methyl-L-thiocitrulline) and a genetic model (eNOS-/- mice). We will further assess vascular smooth muscle function via pressure myography on isolated middle cerebral arteries, measuring constriction to L-NAME in the presence of a NOS inhibitor cocktail and a NO scavenger. Proteomic analysis of vascular tissue will identify potential off-target protein binding.
proposals.contribution: This work will rigorously define the specificity of a key pharmacological tool, clarifying the precise role of endothelial NO in hypercapnic vasodilation. It will provide a refined experimental framework for studying neurovascular aging and disease, directly informing the pathobiology of vascular cognitive impairment by ensuring accurate mechanistic attribution in models of endothelial dysfunction.
proposals.novelty: While L-NAME's primary action is well-known, a systematic investigation of its off-target vascular effects in the context of CO2 reactivity—a critical clinical biomarker—is lacking. This proposal moves beyond the assumption of specificity to directly test it, integrating in vivo hemodynamics with ex vivo vascular biology and proteomics.

proposals.title: Nitric Oxide Modulation of Hypocapnic Cerebral Vasoconstriction: Implications for Vascular Cognitive Impairment
proposals.motivation: Cerebrovascular reactivity to CO2 is a critical homeostatic mechanism, and its impairment is a hallmark of vascular cognitive impairment and dementia. While hypercapnic vasodilation is known to be NO-dependent, the role of NO in hypocapnic vasoconstriction remains poorly defined. Understanding this inverse mechanism is essential for a complete picture of neurovascular coupling deficits in aging and hypertension, conditions where NO bioavailability is altered.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hypocapnic vasoconstriction is actively modulated by basal nitric oxide (NO) tone, such that NOS inhibition will augment constriction, while NO donation will attenuate it, revealing a dynamic regulatory role for NO beyond its established function in vasodilation.
proposals.method: We will use laser speckle contrast imaging and arterial spin labeling MRI in awake, head-fixed mice (including hypertensive and ApoE4 models) to measure cerebral blood flow during controlled hypocapnia. Pharmacological manipulation of the NO pathway will be achieved via systemic administration of the non-selective NOS inhibitor L-NAME and the NO donor sodium nitroprusside. Vasoconstrictive responses will be quantified in the somatosensory cortex and hippocampus, with parallel experiments assessing endothelial NOS (eNOS) phosphorylation state via western blot of isolated microvessels.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the specific contribution of NO to the vasoconstrictive arm of cerebrovascular CO2 reactivity, a fundamental but neglected aspect of blood flow control. It will provide novel mechanistic insight into how NO dysregulation in aging and vascular disease may lead to exaggerated vasoconstriction and hypoperfusion, thereby contributing to cognitive decline.
proposals.novelty: While NO's role in hypercapnic dilation is well-studied, its function in the inverse, constrictive response is unexplored. Testing this in disease-relevant models (hypertensive, ApoE4) directly links a basic vascular mechanism to the pathobiology of neurovascular aging and cognitive impairment.

proposals.title: Non-Glutamatergic Mediators of Parallel Fiber-Evoked Cerebrovascular Responses: Implications for Cerebellar Blood Flow Regulation
proposals.motivation: The neurovascular unit is critical for brain health, and its dysfunction underpins vascular cognitive impairment and dementia. In the cerebellum, parallel fiber (PF) stimulation robustly increases local blood flow, a response widely attributed to glutamate release. However, this assumption overlooks the potential contribution of co-released neurotransmitters and neuromodulators, creating a significant gap in our understanding of integrated cerebellar hemodynamic control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that PF stimulation evokes cerebrovascular dilation through the coordinated release of both glutamatergic and non-glutamatergic signaling molecules, and that the non-glutamatergic component is functionally significant and mediated by specific purinergic and/or peptidergic pathways.
proposals.method: We will use in vivo two-photon laser scanning microscopy and laser Doppler flowmetry in anesthetized mice to measure PF-evoked vascular responses in cerebellar cortex (lobule Crus I/II). We will compare hemodynamic changes elicited by electrical PF stimulation versus local microinjection of glutamate receptor agonists. Pharmacological blockade (e.g., P2Y12 receptor antagonists, CGRP receptor antagonists) will be applied during PF stimulation to isolate non-glutamatergic components. Experiments will be conducted in wild-type and cell-type-specific (e.g., astrocyte, interneuron) Cre-driver mouse lines to identify cellular sources.
proposals.contribution: This work will redefine the neurochemical basis of cerebellar functional hyperemia, identifying novel vasoactive pathways. It will provide a more comprehensive model of cerebellar neurovascular coupling, with direct relevance for understanding how vascular dysregulation in the cerebellum may contribute to cognitive and motor deficits in aging and cerebrovascular disease.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the long-standing, simplified model that PF vascular effects are purely glutamatergic. It innovatively applies the neurovascular unit paradigm, central to Dr. Iadecola's work on dementia, to the understudied cerebellar circulation to uncover novel signaling mechanisms.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Role of Free Radicals in Amyloid-β-Mediated Cerebrovascular Dysfunction: A Test of the Singular Pathway Hypothesis
proposals.motivation: Amyloid-β (Aβ) is a key driver of cerebrovascular dysfunction in Alzheimer's disease and vascular cognitive impairment, leading to impaired cerebral blood flow regulation and cognitive decline. Pre-2026 work, including from Dr. Iadecola's lab, has implicated reactive oxygen species (ROS) as a critical downstream mediator of Aβ's vascular toxicity. However, it remains unresolved whether ROS are the sole obligatory pathway or if Aβ can exert parallel, ROS-independent effects on the neurovascular unit, a distinction crucial for targeted therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Aβ-induced cerebrovascular dysfunction is not exclusively mediated by free radical production, and that Aβ can impair neurovascular coupling and endothelial function through ROS-independent signaling pathways.
proposals.method: We will use a multi-pronged approach in a murine model of cerebral amyloidosis (e.g., APP/PS1 mice). First, we will employ high-resolution laser speckle contrast imaging and closed cranial window preparations to measure neurovascular coupling and whisker-stimulation-evoked cerebral blood flow responses. Second, we will assess endothelial function via pressurized arteriography of isolated cerebral arteries. These vascular readouts will be performed under three conditions: 1) baseline, 2) after acute intracerebroventricular Aβ oligomer infusion, and 3) after Aβ infusion in mice pre-treated with a potent, blood-brain-barrier-permeable antioxidant cocktail (e.g., combining Tempol and MitoTEMPO) or in transgenic mice overexpressing mitochondrial-targeted catalase. A complete rescue of function by antioxidant treatment would support the singular pathway hypothesis, while a partial or absent rescue would indicate alternative mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively test a long-standing paradigm in AD vascular biology. It will clarify the mechanistic hierarchy of Aβ's vascular actions, distinguishing between a linear ROS-dependent pathway and a more complex signaling network. The findings will directly inform the development of more effective antioxidant or combination therapies for vascular cognitive impairment.
proposals.novelty: While ROS are known mediators, systematically testing their necessity as the *sole* downstream effector of Aβ's diverse vascular actions is a novel and critical challenge to existing models. The combined use of physiological imaging, ex vivo vascular biology, and targeted antioxidant strategies in a relevant amyloid model provides a comprehensive test not previously conducted.

proposals.title: Endothelial Priming is Required for Amyloid-β-Induced Cerebrovascular Dysfunction: A Test of the Indirect Action Hypothesis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work establishes that amyloid-β (Aβ) peptides cause cerebrovascular dysfunction, a key contributor to Alzheimer's disease pathology and vascular cognitive impairment. However, the precise cellular target—vascular smooth muscle cells (VSMCs) versus endothelial cells (ECs)—remains contested, with direct Aβ effects on VSMCs often presumed. Resolving this is critical for developing targeted vascular therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Aβ-induced cerebrovascular dysfunction requires prior or concomitant endothelial activation, and that direct application of Aβ to vascular smooth muscle is insufficient to elicit functional deficits without an intact, responsive endothelium.
proposals.method: We will use ex vivo pressurized arteriography of isolated mouse cerebral penetrating arterioles from C57BL/6 and endothelial-specific Nox2 knockout mice. Vessels will be treated with: 1) Aβ1-40/42 oligomers applied intraluminally (endothelial exposure), 2) Aβ applied abluminally (smooth muscle exposure), and 3) sequential exposures. Real-time diameter changes in response to pressure (myogenic tone) and flow (shear stress) will be quantified. Complementary in vivo studies in APP/PS1 mice will use two-photon microscopy to measure cortical blood flow responses to whisker stimulation before and after acute, localized endothelial disruption via photothrombosis.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively isolate the primary vascular cellular target of Aβ, shifting the paradigm from a smooth muscle-centric to an endothelium-initiated model of dysfunction. It will identify endothelial-specific signaling pathways (e.g., Nox2-derived ROS) as prerequisite therapeutic targets for preserving cerebral blood flow in Alzheimer's disease.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the prevailing model of Aβ's direct action on smooth muscle by experimentally decoupling endothelial and smooth muscle exposures in a controlled physiological system, a approach not comprehensively applied pre-2026.

proposals.title: The Met35 Radical is a Universal Mediator of Aβ-Induced Cerebrovascular Dysfunction Across Diverse Vascular Beds and Pathophysiological States
proposals.motivation: Amyloid-β (Aβ) peptides, particularly Aβ1-42, exert potent vasoconstrictive and pro-inflammatory effects on the cerebral vasculature, contributing to cerebral blood flow dysregulation in Alzheimer's disease and vascular cognitive impairment. The methionine-35 (Met35) residue is implicated in Aβ redox activity and radical formation, but its necessity across different vascular segments (e.g., penetrating arterioles vs. capillaries) and in the context of comorbid conditions like hypertension and ApoE4 genotype remains untested. Determining if Met35 is a universal therapeutic target is critical for developing effective vascular protection strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Met35 radical mechanism is indispensable for all major Aβ-induced vascular pathologies, including impaired neurovascular coupling, increased oxidative stress, and inflammatory activation, across distinct cerebral vascular beds and in models of heightened vascular risk (hypertension, ApoE4).
proposals.method: We will employ high-resolution in vivo two-photon microscopy in awake, head-fixed mice (wild-type, hypertensive, ApoE4-targeted replacement) to measure diameter changes in cortical penetrating arterioles and capillary red blood cell flux in response to whisker stimulation before and after topical application of wild-type Aβ1-42 versus Aβ1-42 with Met35 oxidized to methionine sulfoxide (Met35ox). Ex vivo, we will use pressurized arteriography on isolated middle cerebral and posterior cerebral arteries to assess direct vasoactivity. Concurrently, we will quantify vascular oxidative stress (using dihydroethidium imaging) and microglial P2Y12 receptor activation (a key sensor of vascular damage) in these models following peptide exposure.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively establish whether targeting the Aβ Met35 radical is a comprehensive strategy to rescue vascular dysfunction in Alzheimer's disease or if distinct, Met35-independent pathways dominate in specific vascular compartments or comorbid conditions. It will provide a mechanistic map for tailoring antioxidant or anti-Aβ vascular therapies based on disease stage and patient risk profile.
proposals.novelty: While Met35's role in Aβ aggregation and toxicity is known, systematically testing its indispensability for vascular function across the entire cerebrovascular tree and in high-risk genetic/physiological backgrounds is novel and directly challenges the generality of this single-residue mechanism.

proposals.title: Amyloid-β Potentiates Endothelin-1 and Serotonin-Mediated Cerebral Vasoconstriction: A Novel Mechanism for Neurovascular Dysfunction in Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including from the Iadecola lab, has established that amyloid-β (Aβ) peptides impair cerebral blood flow regulation by potentiating thromboxane A2 (U-46619)-mediated vasoconstriction. However, the cerebral vasculature is regulated by multiple endogenous constrictor pathways, such as endothelin-1 (ET-1) and serotonin (5-HT), which are critical in hypertension and vascular cognitive impairment. It is unknown whether Aβ's deleterious effects are specific to the thromboxane pathway or represent a broader dysregulation of vasoconstrictor signaling, a gap that limits our understanding of neurovascular dysfunction in Alzheimer's disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Aβ peptides, particularly oligomeric Aβ1-42, potentiate vasoconstriction induced by key endogenous mediators ET-1 and 5-HT in cerebral arteries, and that this potentiation involves enhanced signaling through their respective G-protein coupled receptors (ET_A and 5-HT_1B/1D) and downstream Rho-kinase activation.
proposals.method: We will use pressurized arteriography of isolated mouse middle cerebral arteries (MCAs) from wild-type and APP/PS1 mice. Vessels will be pre-treated with oligomeric Aβ1-42 or vehicle, followed by cumulative concentration-response curves to ET-1, 5-HT, and U-46619 (control). To dissect mechanisms, experiments will be repeated with specific receptor antagonists (BQ-123 for ET_A, GR127935 for 5-HT_1B/1D) and the Rho-kinase inhibitor Y-27632. Intracellular calcium dynamics in vascular smooth muscle cells will be assessed using Fura-2AM ratiometric imaging during agonist stimulation with and without Aβ pre-treatment.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the specificity of Aβ's action on cerebral vasoconstrictor pathways, moving beyond the established thromboxane model. It will identify novel receptor-mediated targets (ET_A, 5-HT_1B/1D) and downstream effectors (Rho-kinase) for therapeutic intervention. The findings will directly link Aβ pathology to dysregulation of vasoconstrictor systems implicated in hypertension and vascular cognitive impairment, providing a more integrated view of neurovascular dysfunction in dementia.
proposals.novelty: While Aβ's effect on U-46619 is known, its influence on other major endogenous constrictors like ET-1 and 5-HT is unexplored. Testing this in a direct, comparative functional assay on cerebral arteries represents a novel and systematic investigation into the breadth of Aβ-induced vascular dysfunction.

proposals.title: Identification of the Cerebrovascular Amyloid-β Receptor Mediating Vasoconstriction in Alzheimer's Disease Pathogenesis
proposals.motivation: Amyloid-β (Aβ) directly induces pathological cerebrovascular constriction, contributing to cerebral hypoperfusion and cognitive decline in Alzheimer's disease and related dementias. Despite decades of research, the specific molecular target (receptor or binding site) on vascular smooth muscle or endothelial cells that transduces Aβ's vasoactive signal remains unidentified, representing a critical gap in understanding vascular contributions to cognitive impairment and dementia (VCID).
proposals.hypothesis: A specific, high-affinity receptor or binding complex for Aβ peptides exists on cerebrovascular smooth muscle cells, and its identification will reveal a novel therapeutic target to uncouple Aβ deposition from impaired cerebral blood flow.
proposals.method: We will employ a functional proteomics screen using photoactivatable, cross-linking Aβ40/42 probes on primary mouse cerebrovascular smooth muscle cells (CVSMCs) and human brain vessel extracts. Interacting proteins will be isolated via streptavidin pull-down and identified by tandem mass spectrometry. Candidate receptors (e.g., specific GPCRs, receptor tyrosine kinases, or novel proteins) will be validated using CRISPR/Cas9 knockout in CVSMCs, followed by functional assessment of Aβ-induced calcium flux and vasoconstriction in pressurized cerebral arteriole assays. Spatial co-localization will be confirmed in post-mortem human AD brain sections using multiplex immunofluorescence.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively identify the primary vascular Aβ receptor, providing a mechanistic link between Aβ pathology and cerebral blood flow dysregulation. It will establish a new molecular target for drug development aimed at preserving neurovascular function in AD. The findings will directly inform the pathobiology of neurovascular aging and vascular cognitive impairment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described Aβ's vasoconstrictive effects, no study has successfully isolated the specific vascular membrane protein responsible using an unbiased functional proteomics approach in native cerebrovascular cells. This moves beyond correlative associations to direct mechanistic discovery.

proposals.title: Chronic Sub-pathological Amyloid-β Exposure Impairs Cerebrovascular Reactivity and Promotes Microvascular Rarefaction in a Sex-Dependent Manner
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Iadecola and others established that cerebrovascular dysfunction is a critical early contributor to Alzheimer's disease and vascular cognitive impairment. However, most studies use acute, high-dose Aβ or transgenic models with fulminant pathology, which may not recapitulate the prolonged, low-level Aβ exposure hypothesized in human aging. Understanding the specific vascular consequences of this chronic, sub-pathological exposure is essential to define early therapeutic windows.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic, low-level Aβ exposure, insufficient to cause overt amyloid plaques or neuronal loss, will induce progressive deficits in cerebrovascular reactivity and promote microvascular rarefaction, and that these effects will be modulated by sex and ApoE genotype.
proposals.method: We will use a chronic infusion model in wild-type and ApoE4-targeted replacement mice (male and female), administering soluble Aβ oligomers via osmotic minipump into the lateral ventricle for 8 weeks. Cerebrovascular function will be assessed longitudinally using laser speckle contrast imaging to measure cerebral blood flow responses to hypercapnia and whisker stimulation. Post-mortem analysis will involve high-resolution confocal microscopy of FITC-dextran-perfused brains to quantify capillary density and 3D vascular structure. RNA sequencing of isolated brain microvessels will identify molecular pathways dysregulated by chronic Aβ exposure.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the specific cerebrovascular phenotype induced by chronic, pre-plaque Aβ exposure, distinguishing it from acute or transgenic model effects. It will provide a mechanistic link between subclinical Aβ accumulation and the early vascular dysfunction observed in human studies, identifying novel vascular-centric targets for early intervention in Alzheimer's disease risk.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond acute Aβ effects and late-stage transgenic pathology to focus on the chronic, low-dose exposure paradigm relevant to human aging. It uniquely integrates longitudinal in vivo hemodynamics with detailed microvascular structural analysis and molecular profiling in a sex- and ApoE-genotyped context, directly testing a pre-plaque vascular insult hypothesis.

proposals.title: Long-term N-acetylcysteine treatment rescues neurovascular coupling and cognitive function in a mouse model of amyloid-β cerebrovascular pathology
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including from Dr. Iadecola's group, has established that cerebrovascular dysfunction, driven by oxidative stress and amyloid-β (Aβ), is a critical early contributor to Alzheimer's disease (AD) pathogenesis and vascular cognitive impairment. While antioxidant strategies have shown promise in acute settings, their long-term efficacy in preventing the progressive decline of cerebral blood flow regulation and cognitive decline in models with prominent vascular Aβ pathology remains untested and is a major translational gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic, systemic administration of the free radical scavenger and glutathione precursor N-acetylcysteine (NAC) will prevent the development of neurovascular coupling deficits, cerebral blood flow dysregulation, and associated cognitive impairments in transgenic mice expressing human Aβ specifically in brain vascular smooth muscle cells (Tg-SwDI mice).
proposals.method: We will employ Tg-SwDI mice and wild-type littermates, treated long-term (starting at 6 months, for 6 months) with NAC or vehicle in drinking water. Cerebrovascular function will be assessed in vivo using two-photon laser scanning microscopy and laser Doppler flowmetry to measure neurovascular coupling responses to whisker stimulation and resting cerebral blood flow. Cognitive function will be evaluated using the Morris water maze and novel object recognition tests. Post-mortem analyses will include quantification of vascular Aβ deposits, assessment of oxidative stress markers (e.g., 4-HNE, nitrotyrosine), and evaluation of P2Y12 receptor-mediated microglial function, a pathway previously linked to sex-specific vascular cognitive deficits in Dr. Iadecola's work.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide critical pre-clinical evidence on the therapeutic potential of long-term antioxidant intervention for preserving cerebrovascular health in AD. It will delineate the mechanistic link between chronic oxidative stress, vascular Aβ, and functional hyperemia failure. The findings could directly inform the design of future clinical trials targeting the neurovascular unit in early AD and vascular cognitive impairment.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond acute antioxidant studies to test a clinically feasible, long-term regimen in a model where Aβ pathology is primarily vascular, a key but understudied feature of human AD. It uniquely integrates in vivo vascular physiology, behavioral analysis, and molecular pathways from Dr. Iadecola's prior work on P2Y12 and sex differences.

proposals.title: Differential Cerebrovascular Dysfunction Induced by Aβ Isoforms and C-Terminal Fragments: A Mechanistic Link to Vascular Cognitive Impairment
proposals.motivation: Amyloid-β (Aβ) is a key driver of cerebrovascular dysfunction in Alzheimer's disease and related dementias, but its effects are heterogeneous. Pre-2026 evidence indicates different Aβ species (e.g., Aβ40, Aβ42, pyroglutamated forms) and C-terminal fragments (like Aβ34) have distinct biological activities, yet their specific and comparative potencies in disrupting cerebral blood flow regulation—a core mechanism in vascular cognitive impairment (VCI)—remain poorly defined. This knowledge gap limits targeted therapeutic strategies for vascular contributions to cognitive decline.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific Aβ isoforms and C-terminal fragments exhibit distinct potencies and mechanisms in impairing neurovascular coupling and endothelial function, with Aβ42-derived fragments being particularly deleterious to cerebral hemodynamics.
proposals.method: We will use in vivo two-photon microscopy and laser Doppler flowmetry in awake, head-fixed mice (C57BL/6) to measure neurovascular coupling responses to whisker stimulation following topical application of synthetic peptides: Aβ40, Aβ42, AβpE3-42, and C-terminal fragments (Aβ34, Aβ30). Ex vivo, pressurized arteriography of isolated cerebral arteries will assess direct vasoactive effects. Molecular pathways (e.g., P2Y12 receptor, ROS, eNOS) will be probed using pharmacological inhibitors and transgenic models (e.g., APP/PS1 mice). Data will be analyzed using custom algorithms for hemodynamic response curve quantification and dose-response modeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a rank-order potency of Aβ species in causing cerebrovascular dysfunction, directly linking specific Aβ proteoforms to hemodynamic deficits. It will identify the most pathogenic fragments and their signaling pathways, providing a mechanistic framework for fragment-specific vascular targeting in dementia.
proposals.novelty: While Aβ's vascular toxicity is known, a systematic, side-by-side comparison of the vasoactivity of full-length isoforms and specific C-terminal fragments—particularly in the context of functional hyperemia—is lacking and may reveal novel, potent drivers of vascular cognitive impairment.

proposals.title: Spatial Mapping of Amyloid-β Binding Sites on the Cerebral Vasculature Using FRET-Based Proximity Ligation Assays
