proposals.motivation: Cerebrovascular amyloid deposition is a key pathological feature linking vascular dysfunction to cognitive impairment and dementia, including Alzheimer's disease. However, the precise molecular sites on cerebral arteries where Aβ peptides initially bind and aggregate remain poorly defined, limiting our understanding of how vascular amyloidosis impairs neurovascular coupling and cerebral blood flow. Elucidating these sites is critical for developing targeted interventions to preserve vascular health in aging and neurodegenerative disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Aβ peptides bind to specific, spatially discrete receptor complexes on cerebral vascular smooth muscle and endothelial cells, and that these interaction sites can be visualized and quantified using Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) in conjunction with proximity ligation assays (PLA).
proposals.method: We will employ a FRET-based proximity ligation assay (FRET-PLA) on ex vivo mouse cerebral vessels and human post-mortem brain slices. Vessels will be incubated with fluorescently tagged Aβ40 and Aβ42 peptides (donor fluorophores). Tissue will then be immunostained with antibodies against candidate vascular receptors (e.g., RAGE, LRP1, PrP^C) conjugated to acceptor fluorophores. FRET signals, indicating direct molecular interactions (<10 nm distance), will be captured via confocal microscopy with spectral unmixing. Spatial distribution and intensity of FRET signals will be analyzed using established algorithms (e.g., acceptor photobleaching FRET quantification) to map binding sites along the vascular tree.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution spatial map of Aβ binding sites on the intact cerebral vasculature. It will identify which vascular cell types and specific receptor complexes are primary targets for Aβ interaction. This knowledge will establish a foundational framework for understanding the initial steps of cerebral amyloid angiopathy and for designing therapies to block pathogenic Aβ-vascular binding.
proposals.novelty: While Aβ's presence on vessels is well-documented, directly visualizing and localizing the nanoscale protein-protein interaction sites in situ is novel. Combining FRET with PLA on intact vessels moves beyond co-localization studies to prove direct binding, offering unprecedented spatial and molecular specificity.

proposals.title: Dynamic coupling of perivascular amyloid-β and arteriolar tone in vivo: a microdialysis study in a mouse model of vascular cognitive impairment.
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Iadecola's work, establishes that cerebrovascular dysfunction and impaired amyloid-β (Aβ) clearance via the perivascular glymphatic system are key contributors to Alzheimer's disease and vascular cognitive impairment. However, the dynamic, real-time relationship between local fluctuations in interstitial Aβ levels and the contractile state of nearby penetrating arterioles remains poorly understood, limiting our mechanistic insight into neurovascular coupling failure in dementia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that acute, localized increases in perivascular Aβ40 and Aβ42 levels, as measured by in vivo microdialysis, will induce constriction of adjacent cortical penetrating arterioles, and that this vasoconstrictive effect will be amplified in hypertensive and ApoE4 genetic backgrounds known to confer vascular risk for dementia.
proposals.method: We will adapt high-temporal-resolution microdialysis in the somatosensory cortex of awake, head-fixed mice (wild-type, hypertensive, and ApoE4 models) to collect interstitial fluid (ISF) in 30-minute intervals. Concurrently, we will use two-photon microscopy through a chronic cranial window to measure the diameter of nearby penetrating arterioles. ISF Aβ40/Aβ42 levels will be quantified using ultrasensitive Single Molecule Array (Simoa) technology. Time-series data will be analyzed using cross-correlation algorithms to determine the temporal lag and strength of association between Aβ concentration changes and vascular tone alterations.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, in vivo evidence for a dynamic, spatiotemporally coupled relationship between perivascular Aβ and arteriolar function. It will identify whether Aβ acts as a local vasoactive signal contributing to cerebral hypoperfusion. The findings will bridge the pathobiology of neurovascular aging and Aβ metabolism, offering a novel mechanistic target (the Aβ-vascular interface) for interventions aimed at preserving cerebral blood flow and cognitive function.
proposals.novelty: While microdialysis has been used to measure brain ISF Aβ, and two-photon imaging has visualized vessels, their simultaneous application to correlate real-time Aβ dynamics with vascular tone in disease-relevant models is novel. This approach moves beyond static correlations to establish causality in the interaction between a key Alzheimer's pathogen and the cerebral vasculature.

proposals.title: Human Cerebral Artery Organoids as a Platform to Decipher Amyloid-β-Induced Cerebrovascular Dysfunction in Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: Cerebrovascular dysfunction is a critical early contributor to Alzheimer's disease (AD) and vascular cognitive impairment (VCI), with amyloid-β (Aβ) peptides directly impairing cerebral blood flow regulation. Pre-2026 models, primarily using rodent vessels or 2D cell cultures, fail to recapitulate the complex 3D architecture and cellular diversity of human cerebral arteries, limiting translational insight into Aβ's pathogenic effects on human vascular reactivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineered human cerebral artery organoids (hCAOs), which recapitulate the tunica media and endothelial layers, will exhibit Aβ isoform- and concentration-dependent impairments in vasoconstriction and vasodilation, providing a human-specific model of early cerebrovascular pathophysiology in AD.
proposals.method: We will generate hCAOs from human induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) using a directed differentiation protocol toward vascular smooth muscle and endothelial lineages, guided by TGF-β and BMP4 signaling. Organoids will be matured in a microfluidic bioreactor to enhance vessel-like structure and luminal flow. Vascular reactivity will be quantified via live-imaging of lumen diameter changes in response to vasoactive agents (e.g., endothelin-1, bradykinin) before and after treatment with human Aβ40 and Aβ42 oligomers. High-content analysis will integrate calcium imaging in smooth muscle cells and measurements of nitric oxide production.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first human 3D cerebral artery organoid model for studying vascular reactivity, directly linking Aβ species to specific functional deficits. It will provide a novel, genetically tractable human platform to screen for therapeutics targeting cerebrovascular protection in AD and VCI, bridging a critical gap between animal models and human clinical studies.
proposals.novelty: While brain organoids exist, creating dedicated cerebral artery organoids to study hemodynamic function is novel. This approach moves beyond post-mortem histology or rodent models to directly test Aβ toxicity on engineered human cerebrovascular tissue with functional readouts.

proposals.title: Differential Vasoconstrictive Effects of Amyloid-β Oligomers on Cerebral versus Systemic Vascular Beds: Implications for Systemic Vascular Dysfunction in Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Iadecola's work, has established that amyloid-β (Aβ) peptides induce cerebrovascular dysfunction, impairing cerebral blood flow and contributing to cognitive decline. However, it remains unclear whether Aβ's vasoactive properties are specific to the cerebral circulation or represent a systemic phenomenon. This gap is critical because Alzheimer's disease patients exhibit increased risk of peripheral vascular disease, suggesting Aβ may have broader cardiovascular effects beyond the brain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Aβ oligomers exert direct vasoconstrictive effects on both cerebral and systemic resistance arteries, but the magnitude and underlying signaling mechanisms differ, with cerebral vessels being more sensitive due to their unique neurovascular unit environment.
proposals.method: We will use wire myography to measure isometric tension in isolated, pressurized segments of mouse middle cerebral arteries (MCAs), mesenteric arteries, and coronary arteries. Vessels from wild-type and APP/PS1 mice will be exposed to synthetic Aβ1-42 oligomers. Concentration-response curves to Aβ will be generated before and after pharmacological inhibition of key pathways implicated in Aβ signaling (e.g., reactive oxygen species with Tempol, Rho-kinase with Y-27632, and P2Y12 receptors with ticagrelor). Parallel experiments will assess endothelial function via acetylcholine-induced dilation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, comparative functional assessment of Aβ's impact on major systemic versus cerebral vascular beds. It will elucidate whether Aβ is a circulating factor contributing to systemic vascular risk in AD and identify if therapeutic targets (e.g., P2Y12) effective in the brain also protect peripheral vessels. This could redefine the systemic pathophysiology of AD.
proposals.novelty: While Aβ's cerebral vasoactivity is known, a systematic, mechanistic comparison with systemic arteries is lacking. Testing the hypothesis that differential sensitivity exists moves beyond the neurocentric view and investigates AD as a disorder with potential systemic vascular manifestations.

proposals.title: Caspase Inhibition-Induced Hyperthermia Disrupts Neurovascular Coupling and Exacerbates Ischemic Brain Injury
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work by Iadecola and others has established that cerebral blood flow regulation and neurovascular coupling are critical for cognitive health and are disrupted in vascular cognitive impairment and dementia. The pan-caspase inhibitor z-DEVD.FMK is widely used in neuroscience research to study apoptosis in models of stroke and neurodegeneration, but its reported side effect of inducing hyperthermia is poorly characterized. Since even mild hyperthermia is known to worsen ischemic brain injury, understanding the mechanism and cerebrovascular consequences of this drug-induced hyperthermia is essential for interpreting prior studies and developing safer therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that z-DEVD.FMK induces hyperthermia via a peripheral inflammatory mechanism that disrupts central autonomic control, leading to impaired neurovascular coupling and exacerbated blood-brain barrier dysfunction following cerebral ischemia.
proposals.method: We will use a hypertensive, hypercholesterolemic mouse model (e.g., ApoE4 mice) relevant to vascular cognitive impairment. Core temperature will be monitored via telemetry following z-DEVD.FMK administration. We will assess peripheral cytokine profiles and hypothalamic c-Fos expression to identify the hyperthermia pathway. Using laser speckle contrast imaging and two-photon microscopy in vivo, we will measure cerebral blood flow responses to whisker stimulation (neurovascular coupling) and blood-brain barrier permeability before and after drug-induced hyperthermia, both in naive mice and in a model of transient focal cerebral ischemia.
proposals.contribution: This study will define the physiological mechanism of a common research drug's major side effect and quantify its direct impact on cerebrovascular function. It will provide critical data for interpreting past studies using caspase inhibitors in stroke models and inform the development of more specific anti-apoptotic therapies that avoid detrimental hyperthermic effects, thereby protecting neurovascular health.
proposals.novelty: While hyperthermia is known to be bad for stroke outcomes, the specific link between caspase inhibitor-induced hyperthermia and direct, real-time measurements of neurovascular dysfunction has not been established, merging pharmacology, autonomic physiology, and cerebrovascular biology in a clinically relevant model.

proposals.title: Early Amyloid-β Pathology Drives Cerebrovascular Dysfunction and Hypoperfusion in a Murine Model of Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: The prevailing view in vascular cognitive impairment (VCI) and Alzheimer's disease (AD) research is that vascular risk factors (e.g., hypertension, hypoperfusion) drive AD pathology and cognitive decline. However, this unidirectional model fails to explain observations where cerebrovascular dysfunction appears concurrent with or even after early amyloid-β (Aβ) accumulation. Resolving the directionality of this relationship is critical for understanding disease pathogenesis and for designing effective, stage-specific interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that early, pre-symptomatic Aβ pathology, specifically soluble Aβ oligomers, initiates a cascade of cerebrovascular dysfunction—including impaired neurovascular coupling (NVC) and reduced cerebral blood flow (CBF)—prior to significant neuronal loss or cognitive deficits, thereby inverting the traditional causal model.
proposals.method: We will employ longitudinal, multimodal in vivo imaging in a well-characterized transgenic mouse model of AD (e.g., APP/PS1) from pre-plaque to advanced stages. Using two-photon microscopy through a chronic cranial window, we will directly measure NVC (via arteriolar dilation in response to whisker stimulation) and capillary red blood cell velocity. Simultaneously, we will quantify parenchymal and vascular Aβ species (using FRET-based sensors and immunohistochemistry) and correlate these with laser speckle contrast imaging (LSCI) measurements of baseline CBF. Pharmacological (e.g., P2Y12 receptor antagonism) and genetic interventions will be used to dissect molecular pathways linking Aβ to vascular dysfunction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct, longitudinal evidence for Aβ-induced vascular dysfunction as a primary pathogenic event. It will identify the specific Aβ species and molecular pathways (e.g., purinergic signaling) responsible, offering novel therapeutic targets aimed at preserving vascular function early in AD. The findings could fundamentally reframe the neurovascular hypothesis of AD.
proposals.novelty: This proposal directly challenges the canonical sequence of events by testing if Aβ pathology is a cause, not merely a consequence, of vascular failure. It leverages Dr. Iadecola's expertise in NVC and P2Y12 signaling to mechanistically link a defining AD lesion to vascular pathophysiology from the earliest disease stages.

proposals.title: Molecular Crosstalk at the Neurovascular Unit: P2Y12 Receptor Signaling in Amyloid-β Clearance and Blood-Brain Barrier Integrity in ApoE4 Carriers
proposals.motivation: Vascular dysfunction and impaired amyloid-β (Aβ) clearance are co-conspirators in Alzheimer's disease (AD) pathogenesis, particularly in high-risk ApoE4 carriers. The molecular mechanisms linking these pathways at the blood-brain barrier (BBB) remain poorly defined, limiting targeted interventions. This gap is critical as vascular contributions to cognitive impairment and dementia (VCID) and AD converge at the neurovascular unit.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the platelet/ microglial P2Y12 receptor, a key regulator of cerebral blood flow and inflammation, directly modulates Aβ clearance pathways (e.g., LRP1) and tight junction integrity in brain endothelial cells, and that this crosstalk is dysregulated by the ApoE4 genotype and sex hormones.
proposals.method: We will use a multi-scale approach: 1) In vivo, longitudinal two-photon microscopy in awake, behaving ApoE4-targeted replacement mice (male and perimenopausal female) treated with P2Y12 inhibitor (clopidogrel) or vehicle to quantify dynamic Aβ clearance and BBB leakage using fluorescent tracers. 2) Ex vivo, single-nucleus RNA sequencing of sorted brain endothelial cells and perivascular macrophages from these cohorts to map P2Y12-associated transcriptional networks. 3) In vitro, using a human iPSC-derived BBB model with ApoE4 isogenic lines, we will employ CRISPRi to knockdown P2Y12 and measure transendothelial electrical resistance, Aβ transcytosis, and phosphoproteomic signaling to LRP1 and claudin-5.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel molecular link between a vascular signaling receptor (P2Y12) and canonical AD clearance pathways, providing a mechanistic explanation for the cognitive benefits of P2Y12 inhibition observed in prior studies. It will yield a high-resolution map of ApoE4- and sex-dependent transcriptional changes in the neurovascular unit, identifying new therapeutic targets for vascular cognitive impairment.
proposals.novelty: While P2Y12's role in microglial motility and vascular reactivity is known, its direct cell-autonomous impact on endothelial Aβ clearance machinery and tight junctions is unexplored. Integrating dynamic in vivo imaging with cell-type-specific omics in ApoE4 models directly bridges Iadecola's vascular expertise with the molecular pathobiology of AD.

proposals.title: Direct Assessment of Anesthetic Modulation on Cerebellar Climbing Fiber-Evoked Neurovascular Coupling in Awake, Head-Fixed Mice
proposals.motivation: Neurovascular coupling (NVC) is fundamental for brain health, and its dysfunction is a core feature of vascular cognitive impairment and dementia. Most foundational NVC studies, including those on cerebellar climbing fiber responses, have been conducted under anesthesia, which is known to suppress neural activity and cerebral blood flow. This creates a critical gap in understanding true physiological NVC, as the influence of common anesthetics like halothane on specific, well-defined neural circuits remains unclear, potentially confounding models of vascular pathophysiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that halothane anesthesia significantly attenuates the magnitude and alters the temporal dynamics of the climbing fiber-evoked cerebral blood flow response compared to the awake state, due to anesthesia-induced suppression of neural and vascular tone.
proposals.method: We will use chronically implanted cranial windows over the cerebellar Crus II in C57BL/6 mice. Mice will be trained for head-fixation and acclimated to the imaging setup. Climbing fibers will be selectively stimulated via optogenetic activation of inferior olive neurons (using AAV-ChR2 injections) in awake mice, with concurrent laser speckle contrast imaging to measure local cerebral blood flow. The same mice will then be studied under light halothane anesthesia (1.0-1.2%), allowing direct within-subject comparison of evoked hemodynamic responses. Data analysis will compare response amplitude, latency, and spatial spread between states using paired t-tests and generalized linear mixed models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct quantification of how a volatile anesthetic modulates a defined cerebellar NVC pathway in vivo. It will establish a more accurate baseline for physiological NVC, critical for interpreting pathological studies in disease models. The findings will inform the design and interpretation of future neurovascular research, clarifying the limitations of anesthetized preparations.
proposals.novelty: While anesthesia is known to affect global CBF and sensory-evoked responses, its specific impact on the well-characterized climbing fiber NVC pathway has not been systematically tested in a within-subject, awake vs. anesthetized paradigm. This directly tests a fundamental but unverified assumption in neurovascular biology.

proposals.title: Sustained Functional Hyperemia as a Driver of Angiogenesis and Vascular Remodeling in the Aging Brain
proposals.motivation: Functional hyperemia, the rapid coupling of cerebral blood flow to neuronal activity, is a cornerstone of brain health and is impaired in aging and vascular cognitive impairment (VCI). While its acute mechanisms are studied, it is unknown if chronic, repeated hyperemic challenges can induce lasting structural adaptations in the cerebrovasculature. This gap is critical because understanding if and how functional demands can remodel vascular networks could reveal new therapeutic targets to bolster brain resilience against dementia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sustained, repeated episodes of functional hyperemia, beyond inducing transient flow increases, will trigger angiogenesis and increase microvascular density in the activated brain region, and that this adaptive response is impaired in models of neurovascular aging and hypertension.
proposals.method: We will use chronic, bilateral whisker stimulation in awake, head-fixed mice (young, aged, and hypertensive ApoE4 models) to induce repeated functional hyperemia in the barrel cortex over weeks. Cerebral blood flow will be monitored longitudinally using laser speckle contrast imaging. Vascular density, pericyte coverage, and angiogenic markers (e.g., VEGF, CD31) will be quantified post-mortem via high-resolution confocal microscopy of perfused vessel casts and immunofluorescence. Cognitive outcomes will be assessed with tactile-dependent learning tasks. The role of key pathways (e.g., P2Y12 receptor, NO signaling) will be tested using pharmacological inhibitors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct link between sustained functional hyperemia and structural vascular plasticity, defining a novel mechanism by which neural activity maintains vascular health. It will determine how this mechanism fails in aging and hypertension, providing a new framework for understanding the vascular basis of cognitive decline and identifying P2Y12 or related signaling as potential targets for enhancing vascular resilience.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on acute hyperemic signaling, this proposal uniquely investigates the long-term structural consequences of repeated hyperemia, testing the non-obvious premise that functional blood flow regulation is a direct driver of vascular network architecture over time, especially in disease models relevant to VCI.

proposals.title: Isoform-Specific Nitric Oxide Synthase Mediation of Climbing Fiber-Evoked Cerebellar Hyperemia: Implications for Vascular Cognitive Impairment
proposals.motivation: Neurovascular coupling (NVC), the tight link between neuronal activity and cerebral blood flow (CBF), is fundamental for brain health and is impaired in vascular cognitive impairment and dementia (VCID). The climbing fiber (CF) system provides a powerful, well-defined glutamatergic input to the cerebellum, but the specific nitric oxide synthase (NOS) isoforms (neuronal, nNOS; endothelial, eNOS; inducible, iNOS) mediating its robust hemodynamic response remain unresolved. Determining this isoform-specific mechanism is critical for understanding how NVC dysregulation in disease states, such as hypertension and aging, contributes to cognitive decline.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that climbing fiber-induced CBF increases are primarily mediated by neuronal nitric oxide synthase (nNOS), with a secondary, modulatory role for endothelial NOS (eNOS), and that this specific pathway is vulnerable to age- and hypertension-related endothelial dysfunction.
proposals.method: We will use laser Doppler flowmetry or two-photon microscopy to measure CBF in the cerebellar cortex of anesthetized mice in vivo. Climbing fiber activation will be evoked by electrical stimulation of the inferior olive. We will employ a combination of pharmacological inhibitors (e.g., 7-NI for nNOS, L-NIO for eNOS) and genetically modified mouse models (nNOS-/-, eNOS-/-) to dissect isoform contributions. Cerebellar blood flow responses will be quantified before and after selective NOS blockade, and neuronal activity will be confirmed via local field potential recordings.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively map the NOS isoform responsible for a major cerebellar neurovascular pathway, a significant gap in cerebrovascular physiology. It will provide a mechanistic foundation for understanding how hypertension and aging, known to impair endothelial function, might selectively disrupt specific NVC pathways, thereby informing targeted therapeutic strategies for VCID.
proposals.novelty: While NOS roles in cortical hyperemia are studied, the specific isoform mediating the powerful, behaviorally relevant climbing fiber input is unknown. This proposal directly tests this in a defined circuit, moving beyond correlative studies to causal manipulation of specific NOS isoforms in a vascular cognitive impairment-relevant context.

proposals.title: Metabotropic Glutamate Receptor-Mediated Neurovascular Coupling in the Cerebellar Cortex: A Novel Pathway in Vascular Cognitive Impairment
proposals.motivation: Neurovascular coupling (NVC), the tight link between neuronal activity and local cerebral blood flow, is fundamental for brain health and is impaired in vascular cognitive impairment and dementia. While ionotropic glutamate receptors are known mediators of NVC, the role of metabotropic glutamate receptors (mGluRs), particularly in response to specific neuronal pathways like cerebellar climbing fibers, remains poorly understood. Elucidating this pathway is critical for understanding the vascular pathophysiology of cognitive decline.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that climbing fiber stimulation in the cerebellar cortex triggers a component of neurovascular coupling that is mediated by group I metabotropic glutamate receptors (mGluR1/5), and that this pathway is dysregulated in mouse models of vascular cognitive impairment.
proposals.method: We will use in vivo two-photon laser scanning microscopy in anesthetized mice (wild-type and hypertensive ApoE4 models) to image cerebellar capillary diameter and red blood cell flux. Climbing fibers will be selectively stimulated via optogenetic activation of inferior olive neurons (using ChR2). Pharmacological blockade (e.g., with MPEP and LY367385) and allosteric modulation of mGluR1/5 will be applied locally via cranial window to dissect their contribution to the hemodynamic response. Parallel experiments will measure Purkinje cell dendritic calcium transients (GCaMP) to correlate neuronal activity with vascular responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify a novel mGluR-dependent mechanism of neurovascular coupling in the cerebellum, a region implicated in cognitive processing. It will determine if this pathway is compromised in a pre-clinical model of vascular risk, providing a new mechanistic link between vascular disease and circuit-specific NVC failure. The findings could reveal mGluRs as potential therapeutic targets for vascular cognitive impairment.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond the canonical focus on ionotropic receptors and cortical regions, investigating a metabotropic pathway in the context of a defined cerebellar circuit. Integrating climbing fiber physiology with vascular biology in disease models is a novel approach to understanding the vascular basis of cognitive dysfunction.

proposals.title: Differential Neurovascular Coupling of Climbing and Parallel Fiber Inputs in the Cerebellar Cortex: Implications for Vascular Cognitive Impairment
proposals.motivation: Neurovascular coupling (NVC), the tight link between neuronal activity and cerebral blood flow, is fundamental for brain health and is disrupted in vascular cognitive impairment and dementia. While NVC has been studied in the cerebral cortex, the cerebellum—a key region for motor and cognitive function—remains underexplored. A direct comparison of NVC mechanisms between the two major excitatory inputs to Purkinje cells, climbing fibers (CFs) and parallel fibers (PFs), is lacking, yet it is critical for understanding how distinct circuit computations are energetically supported and potentially differentially vulnerable in disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that climbing fiber activation evokes a stronger, more spatially focused, and pharmacologically distinct neurovascular response compared to parallel fiber activation, reflecting their different synaptic architectures and computational roles in cerebellar processing.
proposals.method: We will use in vivo two-photon microscopy in anesthetized and awake Thy1-GCaMP6f mice to simultaneously measure Purkinje cell dendritic calcium signals (neuronal activity) and cortical blood flow (via Texas Red dextran) in cerebellar lobule V. Climbing fibers will be activated via electrical microstimulation of the inferior olive, while parallel fibers will be activated via a separate electrode in the molecular layer. We will quantify the amplitude, spatial spread, and temporal dynamics of the hemodynamic response. Pharmacological interventions (e.g., local application of P2Y12 receptor antagonist ticagrelor, NMDA/AMPA receptor blockers) will be used to dissect the purinergic and glutamatergic signaling components specific to each pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first direct, quantitative comparison of NVC for two distinct canonical inputs within the same brain region. It will define the cellular and molecular signatures of input-specific hemodynamic responses, providing a foundational framework for interpreting cerebellar functional imaging. The findings will inform how selective vascular dysregulation could contribute to cerebellar dysfunction in hypertensive and aging-related cognitive decline.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized NVC in response to mixed inputs, this proposal systematically isolates and compares the hemodynamic signatures of two anatomically and functionally segregated pathways within the cerebellum, a novel approach to dissecting circuit-specific energy demands.

proposals.title: Conservation of the Glutamate-NO-cGMP Pathway in Climbing Fiber-Mediated Neurovascular Coupling in the Non-Human Primate Cerebellum
proposals.motivation: Neurovascular coupling (NVC) is fundamental for brain health, and its dysfunction is a key mechanism in vascular cognitive impairment and dementia. While rodent studies, including seminal work from our lab, have identified the glutamate-NO-cGMP pathway as critical for climbing fiber-mediated NVC in the cerebellum, it is unknown if this mechanism is conserved in primates. Given the translational importance of primate models for cerebrovascular disease, establishing the core NVC pathways in the primate brain is a crucial step.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the glutamate-NO-cGMP signaling pathway, specifically involving neuronal nitric oxide synthase (nNOS), is the primary mediator of climbing fiber-evoked hemodynamic responses in the non-human primate cerebellum, as it is in rodents.
proposals.method: We will conduct experiments in anesthetized adult rhesus macaques. Using a cranial window over the cerebellar cortex, we will perform simultaneous electrophysiological recording of climbing fiber activity (via complex spike monitoring in Purkinje cells) and laser speckle contrast imaging to measure cerebral blood flow (CBF). Pharmacological agents (e.g., the nNOS inhibitor 7-NI, the sGC inhibitor ODQ, and the NO scavenger PTIO) will be applied topically or via microinjection to selectively block components of the pathway. Climbing fiber activity will be evoked via electrical stimulation of the inferior olive.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the translational relevance of a fundamental NVC mechanism from rodents to primates. It will provide critical pre-clinical data on the conservation of cerebrovascular control pathways, informing the development of therapies for vascular cognitive impairment that target NVC. The findings will establish a primate model for studying NVC dysfunction in aging and neurodegenerative disease.
proposals.novelty: While the pathway is well-defined in rodents, this is the first direct investigation of the specific molecular mediators of climbing fiber NVC in the primate brain, bridging a key gap between basic rodent physiology and human cerebrovascular biology.

proposals.title: Defining the Isoform-Specific Contribution of Nitric Oxide Synthases to Functional Hyperemia in the Aging Brain
proposals.motivation: Functional hyperemia, the coupling of cerebral blood flow to neuronal activity, is critical for brain health and is impaired in aging and vascular cognitive impairment (VCI). While neuronal nitric oxide synthase (nNOS) is implicated, the specific roles of endothelial (eNOS) and inducible (iNOS) isoforms remain ambiguous. Clarifying this is essential for understanding the pathophysiology of neurovascular aging and developing targeted therapies for VCI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that despite the efficacy of the nNOS inhibitor 7-nitroindazole, eNOS and iNOS isoforms contribute significantly to functional hyperemia, particularly in the context of aging and cerebrovascular risk factors, and that their relative contributions are sex-dependent.
proposals.method: We will use in vivo two-photon laser scanning microscopy and laser Doppler flowmetry in awake, head-fixed mice (wild-type, eNOS-/-, iNOS-/-, and nNOS-/-) to measure whisker stimulation-evoked parenchymal arteriole dilation and cortical blood flow. Pharmacological inhibition (7-nitroindazole for nNOS, L-NIO for eNOS, 1400W for iNOS) will be applied in young and aged mice of both sexes. Data will be analyzed using custom algorithms for vessel diameter tracking and flow analysis, with statistical modeling to parse isoform- and sex-specific contributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively map the isoform-specific NOS contributions to functional hyperemia, identifying potential compensatory pathways when nNOS is inhibited. It will provide a mechanistic foundation for understanding sex differences in neurovascular coupling decline and inform the development of isoform-specific NOS modulators for preserving cerebral blood flow in dementia.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond the established role of nNOS to systematically test the non-redundant functions of all three NOS isoforms in vivo, integrating aging and sex as critical biological variables—an approach not comprehensively undertaken in prior neurovascular coupling research.

proposals.title: Cerebellar Neurovascular Coupling at the Microscale: Dissecting Single-Vessel Hemodynamic Responses to Parallel Fiber Stimulation Under Nitric Oxide Synthase Blockade
proposals.motivation: Neurovascular coupling (NVC) is fundamental for brain health, and its dysfunction is a key feature of vascular cognitive impairment and dementia. While cortical NVC has been studied, the hemodynamic regulation of the cerebellum—a region critical for motor and cognitive function—remains poorly understood at the level of individual microvessels. This gap limits our ability to define the precise vascular pathophysiology underlying cerebellar contributions to cognitive decline.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in the cerebellum, parallel fiber (PF) stimulation evokes distinct, stimulus-locked diameter changes in individual penetrating arterioles and capillaries, and that these responses are partially independent of nitric oxide (NO) signaling, revealing a unique, region-specific neurovascular control mechanism.
proposals.method: We will use high-resolution two-photon laser scanning microscopy in vivo in anesthetized C57BL/6 mice expressing fluorescent reporters in vascular smooth muscle (e.g., SMA-GCaMP) and endothelium. A cranial window will be placed over cerebellar Crus I/II. Electrical stimulation of PFs will be delivered via a microelectrode. Vessel diameter changes in identified capillaries and arterioles will be measured in real-time using a custom line-scan analysis algorithm in ImageJ/FIJI, tracking fluorescence intensity changes at vessel walls. Experiments will be repeated following local application of the non-selective NOS inhibitor L-NAME to assess NO-independent components.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative map of single-vessel hemodynamic responses in the cerebellum, establishing a baseline for cerebellar NVC. It will identify the specific vascular segments (arterioles vs. capillaries) responsible for functional hyperemia in this region and delineate the contribution of NO to these responses, informing models of cerebellar blood flow regulation in health and disease.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Iadecola's lab has extensively studied cortical and hippocampal NVC, this proposal shifts focus to the cerebellum and scales down to the resolution of single microvessels, a level of analysis not yet applied to this brain region under controlled physiological stimulation.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Isoform-Specificity of L-NAME in Cerebral Blood Flow and Cognitive Impairment: A Role for Endothelial NOS in Vascular Dementia Models
proposals.motivation: L-NAME (Nω-Nitro-L-arginine methyl ester) is widely used as a pan-nitric oxide synthase (NOS) inhibitor to study the role of NO in cerebral blood flow and cognitive function. However, its relative specificity for neuronal (nNOS) versus endothelial (eNOS) isoforms in vivo is poorly defined, leading to potential misinterpretation of vascular versus neuronal contributions in models of vascular cognitive impairment (VCI). Clarifying this is critical for understanding the distinct pathophysiological roles of NO synthases in neurovascular aging and dementia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the chronic cerebral hypoperfusion and cognitive deficits induced by systemic L-NAME administration in mouse models of VCI are primarily mediated by the inhibition of endothelial NOS (eNOS), not neuronal NOS (nNOS).
proposals.method: We will use a multi-modal approach in hypertensive ApoE4 mice, a model relevant to VCI. First, we will chronically administer L-NAME and compare outcomes to mice treated with the more nNOS-specific inhibitor, Nω-Propyl-L-arginine (NPA). Cerebral blood flow will be measured longitudinally using laser speckle contrast imaging and arterial spin labeling MRI. Cognitive function will be assessed via the Morris water maze and novel object recognition. Post-mortem, we will quantify regional NO production, eNOS/nNOS activity via enzymatic assays, and neurovascular unit integrity using immunohistochemistry (collagen IV, GFAP, Iba1). Pharmacological rescue experiments will use the selective eNOS agonist AVE9488.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively attribute the vascular and cognitive effects of L-NAME to eNOS inhibition, refining the interpretation of decades of prior studies. It will clarify the specific contribution of endothelial-derived NO to cerebral hypoperfusion and cognitive decline in VCI, informing more targeted therapeutic strategies aimed at the neurovascular unit.
proposals.novelty: While L-NAME's non-selectivity is acknowledged, its effects in chronic VCI models are routinely attributed to nNOS blockade. This study directly tests that attribution using isoform-selective pharmacology and integrates pre-2026 methods from Dr. Iadecola's lab (cerebral blood flow, cognitive testing in ApoE4 models) to resolve a fundamental pharmacological ambiguity in neurovascular research.

proposals.title: Circuit Mapping of Cerebellar Fastigial Nucleus Control of Cortical Neurovascular Coupling
proposals.motivation: The cerebellum, via the fastigial nucleus (FN), is known to exert powerful, widespread effects on cerebral blood flow, a process termed 'fastigial pressor response.' However, the precise polysynaptic neural pathway linking the FN to cortical microvessels remains a critical gap. Mapping this circuit is essential to understand how central autonomic commands from the cerebellum integrate with local neurovascular coupling to regulate cerebral perfusion, which is fundamental to vascular cognitive impairment and dementia pathologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the FN modulates cortical blood flow via a disynaptic pathway through specific subcortical autonomic nuclei (e.g., parabrachial complex, locus coeruleus) that subsequently influence cortical vasomotor control through noradrenergic and cholinergic projections to perivascular nerves and parenchymal astrocytes.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-modal viral tracing strategy in mice. First, we will inject a Cre-dependent, anterograde trans-synaptic AAV (AAV1-Cre) into the FN of Ai14 reporter mice to label postsynaptic neurons. Second, we will inject retrograde tracers (e.g., cholera toxin B subunit) into the frontal cortex and parietal cortex to identify cortical-projecting neurons receiving FN input. Third, we will use fiber photometry to record calcium activity in FN terminals and candidate relay nuclei (e.g., locus coeruleus) during cortical hemodynamic changes measured with laser speckle contrast imaging, establishing functional connectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive structural and functional map of the FN-to-cortex cerebrovascular pathway. It will identify novel subcortical nodes that translate cerebellar output into cortical blood flow regulation, offering new mechanistic insights into how central autonomic dysregulation may contribute to impaired cerebral perfusion in vascular cognitive impairment.
proposals.novelty: While FN's global vascular effects are known, the specific intervening neural circuitry is unknown. This proposal moves beyond correlative hemodynamic observations to definitively trace the causative neural pathway, integrating state-of-the-art viral genetics with in vivo functional imaging in a vascular neuroscience context.

proposals.title: Impaired Neurovascular Coupling via the P2Y12 Receptor Pathway in Hypertensive Stroke: A Sex- and Age-Dependent Mechanism of Cognitive Decline
proposals.motivation: Vascular cognitive impairment (VCI) and post-stroke dementia are major burdens with no specific treatments. Pre-2026 work, including from the Iadecola lab, shows that neurovascular coupling (NVC), the critical process linking neuronal activity to increased cerebral blood flow, is disrupted in hypertension and aging, contributing to cognitive decline. The P2Y12 receptor (P2Y12R) on perivascular microglia is a newly identified, essential component of this vasodilatory pathway, but its integrity in clinically relevant models of combined hypertension and ischemic stroke remains unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the P2Y12R-mediated vasodilatory pathway is severely compromised in a hypertensive stroke model, and that this impairment is more pronounced in aged and female (particularly perimenopausal) subjects, directly contributing to exacerbated post-stroke cognitive deficits.
proposals.method: We will use established hypertensive mouse models (e.g., AngII-infused or BPH/2) subjected to transient middle cerebral artery occlusion (tMCAO). In vivo two-photon microscopy through a cranial window will be used to directly measure cortical arteriolar diameter changes in response to whisker stimulation (NVC) pre- and post-stroke. This will be combined with local pharmacological manipulation (P2Y12R agonists/antagonists) and genetic targeting of microglial P2Y12R. Behavioral tests (e.g., novel object recognition, Y-maze) will assess cognitive function. Experiments will be conducted in young/aged males and perimenopausal females to determine sex and age interactions.
proposals.contribution: This study will define the specific vulnerability of a key cellular pathway for cerebral blood flow control in a high-risk disease combination (hypertension + stroke). It will provide mechanistic insight into the accelerated cognitive decline seen post-stroke in hypertensive patients and identify P2Y12R signaling as a potential therapeutic target for VCI. The work will also extend the understanding of sex differences in cerebrovascular resilience.
proposals.novelty: While P2Y12R's role in NVC is known, its function has not been tested in the dynamic context of an acute ischemic insult superimposed on chronic hypertension. The integrated examination of age, sex, and the peri-infarct penumbra environment represents a novel, clinically translational approach to understanding post-stroke dementia mechanisms.

ideas.title: Direct Neurovascular Coupling: Testing the Astrocyte Relay Hypothesis via a Cardiac Pacemaker Analogy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that astrocytes are necessary intermediaries for neurovascular coupling by drawing an analogy from cardiac electrophysiology. In that field, the necessity of the sinoatrial node as the primary pacemaker was proven by its targeted ablation, which abolished the coordinated heart rhythm. Adapting this solution, the research would employ precise genetic tools to ablate or inhibit astrocyte signaling specifically (e.g., IP3R2 knockout or chemogenetic silencing) in vivo. The core experiment involves measuring cerebral blood flow responses to neuronal activation (via sensory stimuli or direct neural stimulation) in the absence of functional astrocyte calcium signaling. If the flow increase is completely abolished, it confirms astrocytes as a necessary relay. If it is only modulated or remains intact, it demonstrates that neurons can initiate vasoactive signals directly, with astrocytes playing a modulatory rather than an obligatory role.

ideas.title: Isolating the direct neurovascular contribution: A synaptic transmission-independent assay using extrasynaptic receptor activation and circuit silencing.
ideas.description: This study aims to definitively test whether direct neuronal or astrocyte activation, in the complete absence of functional synaptic transmission and network recruitment, can evoke local hemodynamic responses. The core method combines targeted pharmacological silencing of synaptic vesicle release with highly precise, subcellular stimulation via two-photon glutamate uncaging at extrasynaptic receptors on genetically identified neurons. A complementary approach uses simultaneous optogenetic activation of a target cell and rapid inhibition of its synaptically connected neighbors. This system isolates the direct, synapse-bypassing vascular signaling capacity of each cell type, providing a clear answer to the necessity of synaptic transmission in neurovascular coupling.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporally Resolved Neurovascular Coupling: Simultaneous Two-Photon Imaging and Microdialysis for Causal Linkage
ideas.description: This fused approach integrates in vivo two-photon microscopy with concurrent, targeted microdialysis at the same cortical site. It allows for the visualization of capillary red blood cell dynamics and neuronal/astrocytic calcium activity with cellular resolution while simultaneously sampling the local extracellular fluid for vasoactive substances (e.g., glutamate, K+, prostaglandins, nitric oxide metabolites) during sensory or pharmacological stimulation. The key innovation is the precise spatial and temporal alignment of the sampled chemical milieu with the observed cellular and hemodynamic responses, permitting the direct testing of candidate signaling pathways. This creates a closed-loop experimental platform to pharmacologically manipulate the dialysate and observe the resultant changes in neurovascular coupling at the capillary and cellular level in real time.

ideas.title: Causal dissection of neurovascular and neurometabolic coupling dynamics via controlled neural activation
ideas.description: This redesigned approach causally probes the temporal dynamics linking synaptic activity to its vascular and metabolic support systems. It combines precise, millisecond-scale optogenetic control of defined neural populations with simultaneous, high-speed measurement of local blood flow (e.g., via laser speckle contrast imaging) and an indicator of glucose metabolism (e.g., a genetically encoded FRET glucose sensor). By applying controlled, brief neural stimuli, the system can determine the exact latency, gain, and potential decoupling of the hemodynamic response relative to the metabolic demand, directly testing models of neurovascular coupling. This moves beyond correlation to establish a causal sequence and quantitative relationship between neural activity and its downstream physiological effects.

ideas.title: Simultaneous cell-type-specific voltage and calcium imaging to disambiguate neural correlates of neurovascular coupling
ideas.description: This approach co-expresses a genetically encoded voltage indicator (e.g., ASAP-family) and a red-shifted calcium indicator (e.g., jRGECO1a) in the same defined neuronal population using a single viral vector or intersectional genetics. This enables direct, simultaneous recording of fast membrane potential dynamics (spiking and subthreshold activity) and calcium-mediated signaling within the same cells during behavior or stimulation. The combined optical signals are then correlated with high-resolution local cerebral blood flow measurements, allowing the precise decomposition of which specific electrical and intracellular calcium components of neural activity best predict the hemodynamic response. This directly tests the fundamental assumptions linking indirect fMRI signals to underlying neural activity.

ideas.title: Using Neurovascular Coupling Biomarkers to Differentiate Natural from Artificial Sensory Input
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by using an analogy from computational neuroscience and BCI decoding. In BCI research, differentiating natural perception from artificial electrical stimulation required analyzing complex neural population codes rather than simple response magnitudes. Adapting this solution, the research challenges whether hyperemia mechanisms for artificial electrical lip stimulation are the same as for natural tactile stimuli by proposing they are not. It hypothesizes that natural and artificial stimuli produce distinct neurovascular coupling 'fingerprints'—differences in the spatial pattern, temporal evolution, and metabolic signature of the blood flow response. By using high-resolution vascular imaging to capture these biomarkers, the study would move beyond the assumption of equivalent hyperemia and test for fundamental differences in the vascular response mechanism itself.

ideas.title: Interneuron-specific control of single-vessel hemodynamics during cerebellar microcircuit activation
ideas.description: This study will combine targeted optogenetic activation of specific cerebellar inhibitory interneurons (e.g., molecular layer interneurons like basket or stellate cells) with high-resolution in vivo imaging to measure the resulting hemodynamic responses in individual penetrating arterioles, precapillary sphincters, and capillaries. By simultaneously monitoring interneuron activity, parallel fiber stimulation, and single-vessel diameter/flow changes, we can determine if and how distinct interneuron subtypes differentially constrict or dilate specific vessel segments to shape the local blood flow map. This approach will reveal whether inhibitory microcircuits actively sculpt the spatial precision of functional hyperemia, potentially diverting flow within the capillary network to match the exact topography of excitatory activity.

ideas.title: Cell-type-specific genetic dissection of glutamate receptor subtypes in neurovascular coupling
ideas.description: This approach uses Cre-driver mouse lines crossed with floxed glutamate receptor subunit mice to generate conditional, cell-type-specific knockouts of AMPA, NMDA, and metabotropic receptor subunits. In vivo two-photon microscopy is used to simultaneously measure neuronal calcium activity (via GCaMP) and cortical hemodynamics (via Texas Red dextran or OGB-1) in the somatosensory cortex of awake, head-fixed mice during controlled whisker stimulation. By comparing neurovascular coupling metrics (e.g., hemodynamic response function, spatial spread) before and after tamoxifen-induced receptor deletion in specific populations (e.g., excitatory neurons), the study directly identifies which receptor subtypes on which cell types are necessary for translating local neural activity into a functional hyperemic response.

ideas.title: Neural Hemodynamic Coupling as a Phase Transition: Testing Criticality Models from Statistical Physics
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the linear assumption between local field potential (LFP) magnitude and blood flow increase by drawing an analogy from statistical physics. In that field, the solution to understanding non-linear, threshold relationships near phase transitions (e.g., magnetization in response to a magnetic field) involves modeling critical phenomena and measuring power-law scaling exponents. Adapted to neuroscience, the idea is to conceptualize neurovascular coupling as a system potentially operating near a critical point. The research would involve systematically varying evoked neural activity to measure the precise functional relationship between LFP magnitude and hemodynamic response, searching for power-law scaling, divergence of 'susceptibility,' or sharp threshold effects that would indicate a collective, non-linear transition in vascular engagement, akin to a phase transition, rather than a simple linear translation of signal to flow.

ideas.title: Systematic mapping of regional mediators in cerebellar hyperemia using a multi-messenger screening approach
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that nitric oxide (NO) is the main mediator for functional hyperemia in all cerebellar cortical areas by drawing an analogy from plant ecology. In that field, the assumption of a single dominant allelochemical was overturned by systematic, multi-agent spatial screening. Adapting this solution, the research would involve a systematic mapping of different cerebellar regions (vermis, hemispheres, specific lobules) using a multi-messenger screening approach. This entails employing a panel of specific pharmacological inhibitors and genetic models to block not only NO synthesis, but also other candidate pathways like cyclooxygenase products, cytochrome P450 epoxygenase metabolites (EETs), and astrocyte-derived potassium. By correlating spatially resolved measurements of local blood flow and mediator concentrations during neural activation, the goal is to construct a functional map identifying the predominant vasodilatory mediator or combination of mediators specific to each cerebellar sub-region, thereby testing the universality of the NO mechanism.

ideas.title: Systematic pharmacological profiling of NOS inhibitor classes on cerebellar hyperemia
ideas.description: This idea adapts the systematic structure-activity relationship (SAR) profiling approach common in antimicrobial drug discovery to the study of cerebellar hyperemia. In antibiotic development, when a lead compound shows efficacy, researchers test numerous structural analogs to identify optimal potency and specificity. Adapted here, the research would move beyond testing just 'other' nitric oxide synthase inhibitors. It would involve a planned pharmacological screen of multiple, chemically distinct classes of NOS inhibitors (e.g., non-selective L-arginine analogs like L-NAME, selective neuronal NOS inhibitors like ARL-17477 or Nω-Propyl-L-arginine, and others like S-methyl-L-thiocitrulline). The goal is to systematically compare their efficacy in modulating cerebellar blood flow during somatosensory activation, thereby identifying which inhibitor class and specific compounds are most effective and selective for this cerebellar vascular response, while also characterizing any divergent effects on baseline flow or other vascular beds.

ideas.title: Wide-field optical imaging of comparative synaptic-hemodynamic coupling across cerebellum and neocortex
ideas.description: This fused approach applies wide-field optical intrinsic signal imaging (OISI) to directly compare the relationship between a defined synaptic mechanism and the resulting hemodynamic changes in both the cerebellum and the neocortex. The high spatial coverage of OISI allows for mapping the full spatial extent of these neurovascular events across large areas of each region's surface in vivo. By applying the same synaptic perturbation and imaging protocol to both regions, the experiment can determine if the coupling is governed by universal cellular rules or is fundamentally shaped by local circuit architecture and vascular organization. This creates a novel testbed for dissecting the core biophysics of functional imaging signals across major brain divisions.

ideas.title: Adapting Single-Cell Transcriptomic Barcoding to Map Cerebellar Microvascular Activation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the barcoding and deconvolution principle from single-cell genomics to measure blood flow changes in individual cerebellar microvessels. In the analogous field, unique genetic barcodes tag individual cells for pooled analysis, later deconvolved to single-cell resolution. The adapted solution proposes using unique optical or hemodynamic stimulation patterns as 'barcodes' for individual vessels during somatosensory activation. By applying these distinct temporal or spatial tags and then computationally deconvolving the resulting pooled hemodynamic imaging signal (e.g., from two-photon microscopy or functional ultrasound), one can isolate the precise flow dynamics of each targeted arteriole and capillary, overcoming the challenge of simultaneously tracking many individual vessels at high resolution.

ideas.title: Closed-loop causal dissection of neurovascular coupling initiation and sustenance pathways
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea proposes a direct causal test to determine whether glutamate receptor activation is strictly an initiator or also a sustainer of functional hyperemia. The core experiment uses precise, temporally controlled silencing of presynaptic glutamate release at defined time points after sensory stimulation onset, while monitoring blood flow and candidate secondary pathway activity in real time. This system can distinguish between a sustained requirement for glutamate signaling and a purely permissive, trigger-like role, thereby moving beyond pharmacological correlation. The design explicitly tests for the existence of self-sustaining, glutamate-independent metabolic or vasoactive pathways that maintain the hyperemic response.

ideas.title: Quantifying neurovascular coupling during validated low-arousal behavioral states in head-fixed subjects
ideas.description: This study establishes a rigorous protocol to define and measure neurovascular coupling in a controlled awake state, free from both anesthetic and stress confounds. Subjects are chronically implanted with cranial windows and trained over weeks to voluntarily enter and remain calmly head-fixed in a familiarized environment. During experiments, simultaneous multimodal imaging (e.g., 2-photon microscopy, laser speckle contrast imaging) is synchronized with electrophysiology and real-time stress physiology monitors. Data collection is explicitly triggered only during epochs meeting predefined criteria for low arousal and behavioral quiescence, creating a robust and reproducible 'awake baseline' for neurovascular analysis.

ideas.title: Causal interrogation of neurovascular coupling using simultaneous two-photon imaging and cell-type-specific optogenetics
ideas.description: This redesigned system combines in vivo two-photon microscopy for cellular-resolution imaging of capillary red blood cell dynamics and cell-specific calcium signals with integrated, spectrally distinct optogenetic actuators. A closed-loop experimental paradigm is employed where sensory or direct neural stimulation is paired with precise, cell-type-targeted optogenetic inhibition or activation during the imaging session. This allows researchers to move beyond correlation by testing causal hypotheses—for example, determining if suppressing astrocytic calcium transients directly impairs capillary dilation, or if inhibiting specific neuronal subpopulations decouples neural activity from the hemodynamic response. The output is a functional map of necessity and sufficiency within the neurovascular unit, directly linking specific cellular activity to the resulting microvascular dynamics.

ideas.title: Adapting Power Grid Demand-Response Coupling Models to Study Neurovascular-Glucose Coupling
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from electrical power grid engineering, specifically demand-response coupling models, to investigate the temporal and quantitative relationship between increased cerebellar blood flow and glucose utilization. In smart grids, the precise coupling between electricity demand and supply is solved using high-resolution sensor networks (PMUs) and predictive control algorithms. Adapted to neuroscience, the idea is to deploy analogous 'neural PMUs' via simultaneous, high-temporal-resolution imaging of cerebral blood flow (e.g., arterial spin labeling MRI) and glucose metabolism (e.g., dynamic PET with FDG or novel MR-based methods) during cerebellar activation. By applying system identification and control theory models from grid engineering, the research aims to mathematically characterize the neurovascular-glucose coupling's transfer function, response latency, gain, and potential decoupling under varying cognitive-motor loads, providing a quantitative framework for this fundamental physiological relationship.

ideas.title: Employ a dual-sensor system for simultaneous cellular activity and hemodynamic readout
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from structural engineering, where dual-sensor networks are embedded to correlate different physical parameters (like strain and corrosion) in real-time. The analogous problem in neuroscience is correlating cellular activity with its hemodynamic consequence, which is typically done with separate, temporally offset measurements. The adapted solution is to genetically encode a dual-sensor system within the same tissue: one sensor (e.g., GCaMP) reports activity of a specific cell type, while a second, co-expressed sensor (e.g., a pH or blood-flow-related indicator) reports the local hemodynamic response. This enables direct, simultaneous, and cell-type-specific readouts of neurovascular coupling, providing a more precise causal map than previous methods.

ideas.title: Unbiased mapping of neurovascular coupling mechanisms in awake vs. anesthetized states
ideas.description: This study employs a discovery-oriented approach to determine if the fundamental mechanisms of functional hyperemia are altered by anesthesia. In awake, head-fixed mice performing a tactile whisker task, cerebral blood flow and neural activity are measured simultaneously while systematically applying a panel of receptor antagonists targeting known vasoactive pathways (NO, prostaglandins, potassium channels) and a vehicle control. The same protocol is repeated under common anesthetics (e.g., isoflurane, urethane). By comparing the residual, drug-insensitive hyperemia and the pharmacological profiles across states, the experiment can identify if anesthesia suppresses, unmask, or leaves unchanged specific vasodilatory mechanisms active during behavior.

ideas.title: Investigate interneuron subtypes as circuit breakers for neurovascular coupling
ideas.description: This project uses an analogy from electrical power grid engineering, where specialized circuit breakers with different trip characteristics regulate and protect power flow. We adapt this solution to investigate if specific inhibitory interneuron subtypes function analogously as 'circuit breakers' for neurovascular coupling. The core aim is to determine whether different interneuron populations (e.g., defined by molecular markers like parvalbumin, somatostatin, or VIP) possess distinct functional 'trip characteristics'—such as activation thresholds, temporal response profiles, and spatial influence—that allow them to selectively modulate, dampen, or spatially focus the hemodynamic response to parallel fiber activity. This would reveal a sophisticated, interneuron-specific logic for the precise metabolic regulation of neural circuits.

ideas.title: Biophysically-Constrained Bayesian Disambiguation of Cerebellar Activity from Neurovascular Signals
ideas.description: This project develops a robust framework to infer cerebellar neuronal activity patterns from blood flow data by directly addressing the ill-posed nature of the inverse problem. It combines simultaneous, cell-type-specific electrophysiology and laser Doppler flowmetry (or similar) in animal models to create a detailed forward model of how different neural populations drive hemodynamic responses. This biophysical model then serves as a constraint within a Bayesian inversion algorithm, which estimates the most probable underlying neural activity given observed flow, while quantifying uncertainty. The output is not a single magnitude estimate but a probability distribution over potential activity patterns, clearly identifying when the flow signal is ambiguous and cannot be uniquely inverted.

ideas.title: Network Perturbation Analysis of Cerebellar Crus II: A Systems Ecology Approach
ideas.description: This idea adapts a classic systems ecology solution—studying network-wide effects of a targeted perturbation—to the original seed idea of scaling up cerebellar crus II effects. In ecology, introducing or removing a species allows tracing of biomass and energy flow changes through a food web. Analogously, we propose using targeted crus II activation as a controlled perturbation within the brain's structural connectome. The solution is to combine this focal intervention with whole-brain, high-temporal-resolution hemodynamic monitoring (e.g., functional ultrasound) to map the propagation of blood flow changes. This allows us to model the crus II's influence not just on single regions like the thalamus, but on the dynamics and stability of the entire cortico-cerebellar-thalamic network, treating neurovascular coupling as analogous to energy flow in an ecological network.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Sensor Fusion to Decouple Neurovascular Coupling Signals
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from geophysical subsurface imaging. In that field, multiple sensor modalities (seismic, electromagnetic) are fused using joint inversion models to separate signals from different underground features and fluids, despite measuring a combined surface output. Adapted to neuroscience, the research would develop a similar computational fusion framework. It would treat simultaneous, high-resolution time courses of blood flow (e.g., from advanced fMRI) and glucose utilization (e.g., from novel PET or MRS techniques) as independent data streams. Applying adapted joint inversion and signal separation algorithms would allow the precise decoupling of the hemodynamic and metabolic components of the neurovascular response, enabling the direct measurement of their temporal dynamics and causal sequence during evoked synaptic activity.

ideas.title: Causal Mapping of Cerebellar Crus II Network Effects via Integrated Perturbation and Whole-Brain Hemodynamics
ideas.description: This study aims to establish a causal map of how cerebellar crus II activity modulates hemodynamics across the entire brain. Using precise optogenetic control of crus II neurons combined with simultaneous whole-brain functional imaging in rodents, the design isolates direct network effects from global confounds. Concurrent electrophysiological recordings validate the neural activity cascade from crus II to subcortical and cortical targets. The resulting data will delineate the specific, causally driven cerebro-cerebellar circuits underlying observed blood flow changes.

ideas.title: Causal dissection of neurovascular coupling pathways for natural versus artificial somatosensory input
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a simple comparison to causally dissect the neural pathways and mechanisms linking tactile and electrical stimulation to cutaneous hyperemia. It employs a multi-modal framework integrating controlled afferent recordings, selective neural pathway blockade, and precise hemodynamic measurement in parallel human and animal studies. The goal is to construct a detailed model of how artificial stimulation recruits and integrates neural circuits compared to natural stimuli, identifying specific points of divergence in neurovascular coupling. This will determine if electrical stimulation elicits hyperemia through a physiologically faithful pathway or a compensatory, potentially less specific, alternative route.

ideas.title: Adapting Non-Invasive Wearable Monitoring from Cardiology to Study Neurovascular Coupling in Awake Animals
ideas.description: The original idea is to examine neurovascular coupling in awake animals to remove anesthesia confounds. Drawing an analogy from cardiology, which solved the analogous problem of studying the cardiovascular system under real-world conditions by developing wearable, non-invasive ambulatory monitors (e.g., Holter monitors). We adapt this solution to neuroscience by proposing the development of integrated, miniaturized, and mobile sensor platforms for awake, behaving animals. These head-mounted or telemetric devices would combine modalities like cerebral blood flow imaging and neural activity recording into a single lightweight package. This adapted approach allows for the continuous, long-term investigation of neurovascular coupling dynamics during an animal's full repertoire of natural behaviors, capturing physiologically relevant states that are inaccessible in anesthetized or severely restrained preparations, thereby providing a more ecologically valid understanding of the brain's metabolic support system.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Array Analysis for Multi-Focal Two-Photon Imaging of Cerebellar Network Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics, where arrays of sensors are used to locate and track seismic events through time-delay analysis. The analogous problem in neuroscience is tracking the spatiotemporal propagation of neural activation and its vascular correlates within a dense network like the cerebellar cortex at cellular resolution. The adapted solution is to employ a multi-focal two-photon microscopy setup, which acts as a spatially distributed 'sensor array' within the tissue. By simultaneously imaging predefined, discrete locations (e.g., across Purkinje cell layer and molecular layer) during cerebellar activation and applying cross-correlation analysis to the high-speed fluorescence time-series data, one can map the directionality, latency, and coupling of neuronal and vascular events. This method-transfer enables the reconstruction of functional connectivity and hemodynamic wave propagation with cellular precision, which is difficult to achieve with single-point scanning or widefield imaging.

ideas.title: Inferring Cerebellar Activity from Hemodynamics via Seismic Source Inversion
ideas.description: This research adapts the geophysical inverse problem of seismic source inversion to infer cerebellar neuronal activity from blood flow. In seismology, the characteristics of an earthquake are deduced from distant ground motion measurements by using a physical model of wave propagation. Analogously, we propose to develop a detailed biophysical forward model of neurovascular coupling in the cerebellum that simulates the blood flow response to neuronal firing. An inversion algorithm will then be employed to iteratively adjust the parameters of the simulated neuronal activity source until the model's predicted blood flow best matches the empirically measured hemodynamic signal, providing a quantitative estimate of the original, unobserved neural activity magnitude and pattern.

This approach will reveal how neurovascular coupling in the cerebellar cortex is modulated by the animal's behavioral state and attentional focus, moving beyond anesthesia-suppressed coupling to understand the dynamic vascular support of active sensory processing.

ideas.title: Quantitative Profiling of a Synaptic Mechanism's Expression and Tuning Across Brain Regions
ideas.description: This project tests the hypothesis that a synaptic mechanism, while potentially present in multiple brain regions, is quantitatively tuned by local microcircuitry and molecular environments. We perform a systematic comparison using standardized assays for synaptic function and molecular composition in identified, homologous neuronal populations across four distinct brain regions. The analysis quantifies not just presence/absence, but key parameters like induction threshold, stability, and coupling to network oscillations. This approach reveals whether the mechanism is a rigid, universal building block or a flexible component adapted to regional computational demands.

ideas.title: Adaptive Optics Scanning Laser Ophthalmoscopy for Cortical Hemodynamic Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ophthalmology to solve the problem of optical distortion in wide-field brain imaging. In retinal imaging, Adaptive Optics Scanning Laser Ophthalmoscopy (AOSLO) brilliantly solves the analogous problem of obtaining high-resolution images through the distorting optics of the eye by using a closed-loop system with a wavefront sensor and deformable mirror for real-time aberration correction. We propose integrating this adaptive optics technology into wide-field optical intrinsic signal imaging systems used in neuroscience. By dynamically correcting for tissue-induced optical aberrations, this adaptation will transform the method, enabling hemodynamic mapping with dramatically improved spatial resolution and clarity across large cortical surfaces, effectively bridging the gap between wide-field coverage and microscopic vascular detail.

ideas.title: Apply a Chemogenetic 'Dimmer Switch' to Cerebellar Afferent Pathways
ideas.description: The original idea proposed replacing electrical stimulation with optogenetic activation of specific trigeminal afferent pathways for superior cell-type specificity. Drawing an analogy from synthetic biology, where tunable 'dimmer switches' (e.g., DREADDs) are used for graded control of metabolic pathways, this expanded idea adapts that solution. Instead of light, it proposes using chemogenetics: virally delivering designer receptors exclusively activated by designer drugs (DREADDs) to the same trigeminal afferent neuron population. Administration of the inert ligand CNO would then provide a pharmacologically graded, sustained, and reversible 'dimmer switch' control over cerebellar afferent input, enabling novel experiments into how the magnitude and duration of pathway activity modulate crus II processing and cerebellar learning.

ideas.title: Employ genetically encoded NO sensors with subcellular targeting for compartment-specific dynamics

In that field, the problem of resolving spatially confined signaling is solved by targeting genetically encoded fluorescent biosensors to specific subcellular compartments using localization sequences. Adapting this, we propose to engineer NO biosensors (e.g., geNOps or similar) with targeting motifs (like membrane-anchoring or organelle-specific peptides) for expression in specific cell types of the cerebellar circuit. This would allow direct, high-resolution measurement of NO dynamics within critical microdomains—such as at the astrocyte-vascular interface or within specific neuronal populations—during functional hyperemia, revealing which cellular compartments are the primary sources and targets of NO signaling.

ideas.title: Comparative Synaptic Architecture Analysis Using Cross-System Validation
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the comparative methodology from genomics to challenge the assumption that a synaptic mechanism is either universal or cerebellum-specific. Drawing from comparative genomics—where scientists distinguish conserved core mechanisms from lineage-specific adaptations by analyzing homologous sequences across diverse species—we propose a multi-region, multi-species comparative analysis of synaptic architecture. Instead of a simple cerebellum-versus-neocortex comparison, the study would systematically examine the synaptic mechanism across an evolutionary and anatomical spectrum: analyzing it in cerebellum, neocortex, hippocampus, and basal ganglia of mice, rats, and non-human primates. By constructing a 'phylogenetic' map of synaptic traits, conserved features appearing across all regions and species would indicate a universal mechanism fundamental to neural computation. In contrast, traits consistently unique to cerebellar circuits across evolutionary lineages would provide strong evidence for cerebellum-specific specialization, revealing how this brain region's unique computational demands have shaped its synaptic biology.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Array Signal Processing to Isolate Cerebellar BOLD Signals from Systemic Noise
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics to the challenge of mapping cerebellar hemodynamics with BOLD fMRI. In seismology, arrays of sensors and techniques like beamforming are used to isolate weak, localized seismic signals from pervasive background noise. The adapted research idea applies this principle by treating the fMRI receiver array as a sensor network. Advanced post-processing algorithms, inspired by seismic signal processing, would be developed to distinguish the spatially specific BOLD responses in the rodent cerebellum from systemic physiological noise (e.g., global hemodynamic shifts from respiration or cardiac cycles). This method transfer aims to significantly improve the spatial specificity and signal-to-noise ratio of cerebellar functional maps in rodents.

ideas.title: Closed-loop biomimetic optogenetic stimulation of trigeminal afferents for naturalistic cerebellar input
ideas.description: This redesigned approach moves beyond simple 'on/off' optogenetic activation to replicate the natural firing patterns of trigeminal afferent neurons projecting to crus II. A first experimental phase characterizes the precise spatiotemporal activity patterns of these neurons in response to a range of orofacial stimuli using in vivo calcium imaging. These recorded patterns are then used to program a closed-loop controller that drives a multi-channel optogenetic interface, delivering complex, dynamic stimulation sequences that mimic natural afferent signaling. This system ensures that the artificial input to the cerebellum carries biologically relevant temporal and population codes, preserving the integrity of information processing in the cerebellar cortical network.

ideas.title: Adaptive optics from astronomy for enhanced two-photon microscopy in scattering tissue
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of adaptive optics (AO) from astronomical imaging to overcome a key limitation in the original two-photon microscopy method. In astronomy, AO corrects for atmospheric distortion to achieve clear images of celestial objects. The analogous problem in deep-tissue imaging is scattering and optical aberration, which blurs cellular-resolution data. The adapted solution is to integrate an AO system into the two-photon microscope: a wavefront sensor measures distortions using a fluorescent guide star within the tissue, and a corrective element (e.g., a spatial light modulator) pre-distorts the excitation laser beam in real-time to compensate. This will enhance the resolution and signal-to-noise ratio for visualizing capillary dynamics and cell-specific calcium during stimulation at depths previously compromised by scattering.
